notes:
searches: ~14, clusters: 10 (Bohemia/Gregory VII; Huss biography; Wycliffe/Anne/German students; two-Englishmen pictures; summons/interdict; schism/Constance convocation; Huss trial/execution; Jerome trial/execution; Hussite wars/Compactata; United Brethren) + 2 follow-up searches to give each 'disputed' verdict a second independent source. McAdams benchmark: narrative closely follows Wylie's History of Protestantism; attributed quotes cluster to authentic Hus correspondence (82 letters preserved by Peter of Mladonovice, tr. Bonnechose 1846), Mladonovice's Relatio, Poggio Bracciolini's eyewitness letter on Jerome, and Aeneas Sylvius. Anachronistic candidates (Waldenses/Albigenses seedbed, Queen Anne as Wycliffite convert, German students carrying Hus's gospel, reworded Four Articles, Hus's 'century' prophecy) inherited from 19th-c Protestant historiography. Control sample from needs_verification:false: GC-06-020, GC-06-079, GC-06-144 (all smuggle checkable facts into interpretive frames; all verified supported).

defender: searches: ~1; full defenses: 0, cheap audits: 6 (GC-06-005, 010, 025, 034, 162, 174)

Chapter argument reconstruction: Chapter 6 extends the book's continuity thesis from the Waldenses (ch. 4) and Wycliffe (ch. 5) into Bohemia, arguing that a single unbroken current of 'true faith' runs from apostolic Christianity through persecuted remnants into Huss and Jerome, and from them onward toward the Reformation proper (Luther, ch. 7). Its load-bearing claims are the well-documented biographical and trial narrative of Huss (birth, Prague career, Bethlehem Chapel preaching, papal summons/interdict, Council of Constance, martyrdom) and of Jerome (recantation, retraction, martyrdom), all of which the verification/defense stages found solidly supported by primary and secondary sources (Mladonovice, Poggio, Bonnechose's edition of Hus's letters). Around this documentary spine the chapter weaves several connective and rhetorical claims inherited from Wylie/Foxe-style 19th-century Protestant historiography: the Waldensian/Albigensian seedbed reaching Bohemia, Queen Anne as a Wycliffite conduit, the reversed causality of the German students' exodus, a rephrased Four Articles of Prague, and the 'century' prophecy bridging Huss to Luther. None of these five confirmed discrepancies touch the documentary core (Huss's and Jerome's own words and deaths); they are all connective tissue asserting how Bohemia's dissent linked backward to Waldensian/Wycliffite origins and forward to the German Reformation. Consequently the chapter's central narrative (a real reformer persecuted and executed by an institutionally corrupt church, provoking national resistance and a lasting dissenting church) survives subtraction of all five items intact; what is weakened is specifically the SEAMLESSNESS of the continuity thesis that the book's central thesis chain requires between Waldenses/Wycliffe and Huss, and between Huss and the German Reformation. Two of the five (GC-06-034, GC-06-174) bear directly on that inter-chapter linkage and are scored higher on centrality for that reason, even though neither is load-bearing for chapter 6's own internal argument. Consistent with CLAUDE.md rule 4, all five anachronistic items reflect White's fidelity to her stated 19th-century sources (chiefly Wylie) rather than invention: the errors are in the sources she trusted, not fabrications of her own, which matters for the eventual synthesis on authorial method vs. authorial invention.

claims.quote: The gospel had been planted in Bohemia as early as the ninth century.
claims.claim_text: The gospel had been planted in Bohemia as early as the ninth century.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Cyrillo-Methodian mission reached Great Moravia 863; Duke Borivoj of Bohemia baptized c.883.

claims.quote: The Bible was translated, and public worship was conducted, in the language of the people.
claims.claim_text: In early Bohemia the Bible was translated and public worship was conducted in the language of the people.

verification.rationale: Cyril and Methodius created the Old Church Slavonic liturgy and Scripture translation; a Slavonic house survived at Sazava near Prague into the 11th c.

sources.title: Old Church Slavonic in Great Moravia and Bohemia (Brill)

claims.quote: Gregory VII., who had taken it upon him to humble the pride of kings, was no less intent upon enslaving the people, and accordingly a bull was issued forbidding public worship to be conducted in the Bohemian tongue.
claims.claim_text: Pope Gregory VII issued a bull forbidding public worship to be conducted in the Bohemian tongue.

verification.rationale: Gregory VII's letter to Vratislaus II (2 Jan 1080) refused Slavonic/vernacular liturgy for Bohemia; a letter of refusal rather than a formal 'bull,' but the substance is accurate.

claims.quote: The pope declared that “it was pleasing to the Omnipotent that His worship should be celebrated in an unknown language, and that many evils and heresies had arisen from not observing this rule.”(125)
claims.claim_text: Pope Gregory VII is quoted as declaring that it was pleasing to God that worship be celebrated in an unknown language, and that many evils and heresies arose from not observing this rule.

verification.rationale: The 1080 letter genuinely argues it pleased God that Scripture be obscure in places lest, if open to all, it be held cheap or mislead. GC's wording is a loose but fair paraphrase.

claims.quote: Many of the Waldenses and Albigenses, driven by persecution from their homes in France and Italy, came to Bohemia.
claims.claim_text: Many Waldenses and Albigenses, driven by persecution from their homes in France and Italy, came to Bohemia.

verification.rationale: Scattered Waldensian communities existed in German/Bohemian lands, but the mass migration of Waldenses and Albigenses from France/Italy as a Bohemian seedbed of dissent is a Protestant historiographical construction conflating distinct movements.
verification.modern_consensus: Some Waldensian presence attested; Cathar/Albigensian migration to Bohemia is unattested and the proto-Protestant lineage is rejected.
verification.period_historiography: Wylie's History of Protestantism treated Waldenses, Albigenses, Lollards and Hussites as one continuous proto-Protestantism.

defense.argument: The claim splits into a defensible half and an indefensible half. Waldensian presence in Bohemia is genuinely attested: late-14th-century inquisition records (e.g. Peter Zwicker's campaigns of the 1390s) document Waldensian communities in southern Bohemia, and some did arrive fleeing persecution, so 'Waldenses ... came to Bohemia' has a real historical kernel that the verifier itself concedes ('scattered Waldensian communities existed in German/Bohemian lands'). White reproduces her source faithfully rather than inventing the motif. But the other half — a mass migration of Albigenses (Cathars) from France and Italy into Bohemia — is unattested, and the framing of these refugees as a continuous proto-Protestant seedbed that 'preserved the true faith from century to century' into Hussitism is exactly the Wylie continuity-thesis that modern scholarship ('Waldensianism Before Waldo') rejects. Because the Cathar migration is undocumented and the proto-Protestant lineage is a period construction traceable to Wylie, the anachronistic verdict is correctly placed and cannot be lifted to 'supported.'

impact.reasoning: The claim conflates a real, modest Waldensian presence in southern Bohemia with an unattested mass migration of Albigenses (Cathars) from France and Italy, feeding the Wylie-style continuity thesis that these refugees 'preserved the true faith from century to century.' This is the chapter's opening framing device and restates, for Bohemia specifically, the central-thesis-chain claim that persecuted minorities carried the true faith forward across centuries -- so centrality is high. Deleting the sentence, however, does not damage the chapter's own argument: Huss's story is independently anchored in Wycliffe/Jerome transmission (GC-06-024, 026, both supported) and needs no Waldensian/Albigensian pedigree, and the broader continuity thesis is separately established in chapters 4-5. As an anachronistic verdict, the error reflects Wylie's period historiography of treating Waldenses, Albigenses and Hussites as one proto-Protestant lineage, not an invention by White.

claims.quote: But Heaven had provided other agencies for the preservation of the church.
claims.claim_text: Heaven had provided other agencies for the preservation of the true church in Bohemia.

verification.rationale: Theological assertion of divine providence; not empirically checkable.

claims.quote: Thus Rome decreed that the light of God’s word should be extinguished, and the people should be shut up in darkness.
claims.claim_text: Rome's policy of restricting vernacular worship was intended to extinguish the light of God's word and keep the people in darkness.

verification.rationale: Interpretive claim about Rome's intent; not empirically testable.

claims.quote: Before the days of Huss, there were men in Bohemia who rose up to condemn openly the corruption in the church and the profligacy of the people.
claims.claim_text: Before John Huss, men in Bohemia openly condemned corruption in the church and profligacy of the people.

verification.rationale: Pre-Hus reform preachers (Waldhauser, Milic of Kromeriz, Matthias of Janov) publicly condemned clerical corruption in late-14th-c Bohemia.

claims.quote: Driven to worship in the forests and the mountains, they were hunted by soldiers, and many were put to death. After a time it was decreed that all who departed from the Romish worship should be burned.
claims.claim_text: Pre-Huss Bohemian dissenters were driven to worship in forests and mountains, hunted by soldiers with many put to death, and it was later decreed that all who departed from Roman worship should be burned.

verification.rationale: The dramatized picture of pre-Hus dissenters hunted in forests and a blanket burn-decree reflects Wylie's coloring, apparently transferring generic Waldensian-persecution imagery to Bohemia; it is not documented for pre-Hussite Bohemia in the standard accounts, and modern scholarship treats such continuity/persecution motifs as a Protestant construction. Inconclusive either way.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – pre-Hus reform, no such burn-decree recorded
'Waldensianism Before Waldo' (Church History, Cambridge Core) – Protestant transfer of persecution motifs

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure: the specific staging (forest/mountain worship, a blanket 'all who departed shall be burned' decree) is improbable, while the weakened core - real, documented pre-Hus Bohemian persecution, with burning as the actual legal penalty for obstinate/relapsed heresy - rises to well-supported, making this an absoluteness/retrojection failure over a genuine substrate rather than an invented event. Centrality is moderate: the claim opens the chapter's persecuted-remnant preamble linking Waldenses to Huss and touches the book's continuity chain, but it is not load-bearing - delete it and the chapter's own argument (Huss as a documented persecuted reformer) is untouched, since the trial/execution narrative that follows carries the chapter regardless.

claims.quote: One of those who “taught that salvation was only to be found by faith in the crucified Saviour,” declared when dying, “The rage of the enemies of the truth now prevails against us, but it will not be forever; there shall arise one from among the common people, without sword or authority, and against him they shall not be able to prevail.”(126)
claims.claim_text: An unnamed pre-Huss Bohemian teacher who taught salvation by faith in the crucified Savior is quoted as declaring while dying that one would arise from among the common people whom the enemies of truth would not be able to prevail against.

verification.rationale: A deathbed prophecy of a coming reformer, of the same motif as the goose/swan legend; undocumented in contemporary primary sources and locatable only in the 19th-c Protestant tradition GC drew on.
verification.modern_consensus: Reformer-prophecies are treated by modern scholarship as post hoc constructions, not authentic utterances.
verification.period_historiography: Wylie and allied historians reported such sayings as genuine foreshadowings of Huss/Luther.

defense.argument: The verifier's own source undercuts the anachronistic label. The claim is an attributed quotation of an ANONYMOUS dying pre-Huss Bohemian teacher, transmitted via White's 19th-century source (ref. 126, the Bonnechose/Wylie tradition). The verifier's sole cited source is the Jan Hus Wikipedia article on the 'goose/swan' legend — but that legend is Huss's OWN, named prophecy predicting Luther ('you may roast this goose, but a swan shall arise'), a different saying by a different (named) person. A targeted search confirms modern discussion attaches only to Huss's own 'hundred years' prophecy, not to this anonymous deathbed utterance, which no surviving contemporary evidence records at all. 'Anachronistic' asserts a claim is wrong by modern standards but matched 19th-c sources; here modern historiography has not positively judged this saying wrong — it simply cannot confirm or deny a private anonymous deathbed utterance that exists only in the devotional tradition. That is the definition of 'unverifiable', and re-classing it there corrects a verifier over-reach (its own confidence was 'low'). The prophecy's later interpretive framing as foreshadowing Huss is handled separately at GC-06-011 (already unverifiable).

new_sources.title: John Hus (GreatSite) — discussion attaches only to Hus's own '100 years' saying, not the anonymous pre-Hus prophecy

claims.quote: Luther’s time was yet far distant; but already one was rising, whose testimony against Rome would stir the nations.
claims.claim_text: The dying man's prophecy is interpreted as foreshadowing Huss (with Luther's time still far distant).

verification.rationale: Interpretive framing tying a prophecy to Huss and Luther; not testable.

claims.quote: John Huss was of humble birth, and was early left an orphan by the death of his father.
claims.claim_text: John Huss was of humble birth and was left an orphan early by the death of his father.

verification.rationale: Hus's humble/peasant birth at Husinec is well attested, and his father (Michael) apparently died when Hus was very young; the strong 'orphan' framing goes beyond what is documented, since little is known of his parents.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – father died when Hus was young; parents' details scant

claims.quote: His pious mother, regarding education and the fear of God as the most valuable of possessions, sought to secure this heritage for her son.
claims.claim_text: John Huss's mother, regarding education and the fear of God as the most valuable possessions, sought to secure this heritage for her son.

verification.rationale: Modern encyclopedic accounts report that Hus's mother encouraged his education and entry into the priesthood, giving the claim partial corroboration; but her name is unknown and the devotional characterization ('education and the fear of God as the most valuable possessions') rests on late, thinly documented tradition. Genuinely uncertain.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – mother encouraged education/priesthood; her name unknown

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A motive-attribution failure, distinct from the fact-failures in this cluster: the claim asserts a specific inner valuation ('education and the fear of God as the most valuable of possessions') for an unnamed, undocumented woman, while the one attested family-motive datum (Hus's own admission he first sought priesthood for money and prestige) points the other way. The weakened version reaches only genuinely-open - modern specialists are themselves split on whether the mother had any formative role at all - so an unresolved possibility survives, not vindication. Centrality is minimal: this is decorative biographical color establishing a pious-upbringing trope; deleting it changes nothing about the chapter's argument concerning Huss's reform career or martyrdom.

claims.quote: Huss studied at the provincial school, and then repaired to the university at Prague, receiving admission as a charity scholar.
claims.claim_text: John Huss studied at a provincial school and then attended the university at Prague, admitted as a charity scholar.

verification.rationale: Hus studied at the University of Prague, supporting himself as a poor scholar; the 'charity scholar' detail fits his documented poverty.

claims.quote: He was accompanied on the journey to Prague by his mother; widowed and poor, she had no gift of worldly wealth to bestow upon her son, but as they drew near to the great city, she kneeled down beside the fatherless youth, and invoked for him the blessing of their Father in heaven.
claims.claim_text: Huss's widowed mother accompanied him on his journey to Prague and, nearing the city, kneeled beside him to invoke a blessing on him.

verification.rationale: Devotional anecdote (Bonnechose/Wylie); the standard modern accounts of what is known of Hus's early life do not include this kneeling-at-Prague scene, and record that little is documented about his mother. Undocumented, genuinely uncertain.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – early life, episode not attested

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure: the specific devotional scene (widowed mother accompanying Huss to Prague and kneeling to bless him) has zero medieval attestation and traces to the Comenius-via-Wylie martyrological layer shared across this cluster. Even the generalized weakened version ('mother supported and blessed his departure') reaches only genuinely-open, meaning the softened claim remains an unresolved possibility rather than an established fact. As pure sentimental scene-setting it carries no weight in the chapter's actual argument about Huss's reform and trial; deleting it leaves the chapter's substantive claims entirely intact.

claims.quote: At the university, Huss soon distinguished himself by his untiring application and rapid progress, while his blameless life and gentle, winning deportment gained him universal esteem.
claims.claim_text: At the university in Prague, Huss distinguished himself through diligent application and rapid progress and gained universal esteem for his conduct.

verification.rationale: Hus advanced rapidly (BA 1393, MA 1396, dean 1401, rector 1409); his reputation for upright life is standard in the biographies.

claims.quote: On the occasion of a jubilee, he went to confession, paid the last few coins in his scanty store, and joined in the processions, that he might share in the absolution promised.
claims.claim_text: During a jubilee, Huss went to confession, paid his last coins, and joined in processions to share in the promised absolution.

verification.rationale: Anecdote of Hus's early conventional piety (via Bonnechose); consistent with his documented traditional devotion before his reform period but not otherwise corroborated in detail.

claims.quote: After completing his college course, he entered the priesthood, and rapidly attaining to eminence, he soon became attached to the court of the king.
claims.claim_text: After completing his college course, Huss entered the priesthood and rapidly rose to eminence, becoming attached to the Bohemian royal court.

verification.rationale: Hus was ordained priest in 1400, became prominent, and served the royal court, enjoying the favor of Queen Sophia and King Wenceslaus IV.

claims.quote: He was also made professor and afterward rector of the university where he had received his education.
claims.claim_text: Huss was made professor and later rector of the University of Prague, where he had studied.

verification.rationale: Hus was a master at the University of Prague and served as its rector 1409–1410.

claims.quote: In a few years the humble charity scholar had become the pride of his country, and his name was renowned throughout Europe.
claims.claim_text: Within a few years Huss, formerly a charity scholar, became renowned throughout Europe as the pride of his country.
claims.extractor_note: Sweeping reputational claim; borderline biographical but framed as interpretive praise.

verification.rationale: Control-sample item: the praise smuggles a checkable reputational claim. Hus did rise from poverty to national prominence (rector, court preacher, reform leader). 'Renowned throughout Europe' is hyperbole before Constance, but the core is accurate.

claims.quote: Several years after taking priest’s orders he was appointed preacher of the chapel of Bethlehem.
claims.claim_text: Several years after taking priest's orders, Huss was appointed preacher of the chapel of Bethlehem in Prague.

verification.rationale: Hus was appointed preacher at the Bethlehem Chapel in 1402, two years after ordination in 1400.

claims.quote: The founder of this chapel had advocated, as a matter of great importance, the preaching of the Scriptures in the language of the people.
claims.claim_text: The founder of the Bethlehem chapel had advocated preaching the Scriptures in the language of the people.

verification.rationale: Bethlehem Chapel was founded in 1391 expressly for preaching in Czech, so its founders promoted vernacular preaching.

sources.title: Bethlehem Chapel (Wikipedia) – founded 1391 for Czech preaching

claims.quote: But there was great ignorance of the Bible, and the worst vices prevailed among the people of all ranks.
claims.claim_text: There was great ignorance of the Bible and severe vices prevailed among Bohemians of all ranks at the time of Huss's preaching.

verification.rationale: Sweeping evaluative characterization of a population's moral/religious state; not empirically testable.

claims.quote: A citizen of Prague, Jerome, who afterward became so closely associated with Huss, had, on returning from England, brought with him the writings of Wycliffe.
claims.claim_text: Jerome of Prague, on returning from England, brought with him the writings of John Wycliffe.

verification.rationale: Jerome studied at Oxford c.1399–1401 and brought Wycliffe's works (incl. Dialogus, Trialogus) back to Prague.

claims.quote: The queen of England, who had been a convert to Wycliffe’s teachings, was a Bohemian princess, and through her influence also the Reformer’s works were widely circulated in her native country.
claims.claim_text: The Queen of England, a convert to Wycliffe's teachings, was a Bohemian princess, and through her influence Wycliffe's works were widely circulated in Bohemia.

verification.rationale: Anne of Bohemia was indeed a Bohemian princess (queen of Richard II) who valued vernacular Scripture, but 'convert to Wycliffe's teachings' who spread his works derives from the Foxe/Wylie tradition; no evidence she was a Wycliffite, and the main channel to Bohemia was Czech students at Oxford.
verification.modern_consensus: Anne was a pious Catholic owning vernacular gospels; no Wycliffite conversion, and she was not the principal conduit of Wycliffe's works to Bohemia.
verification.period_historiography: Foxe's Acts and Monuments and Wylie presented Anne as a Wycliffite queen diffusing his doctrine.

sources.title: Bohemia in English Religious Controversy (vanDussen, BRRP)

defense.argument: A real kernel exists: Anne of Bohemia was a Bohemian princess and queen of England (wife of Richard II), and she genuinely owned and valued vernacular Scripture — documented as possessing the gospels in Latin, Czech and German, and Wycliffe himself praised her for it in his own writing. Her marriage opened Anglo-Bohemian dynastic and scholarly channels along which ideas travelled. So 'a Bohemian princess who was queen of England and prized vernacular Scripture' is accurate. But the load-bearing assertions — that she was a 'convert to Wycliffe's teachings' and that 'through her influence his works were widely circulated in Bohemia' — go beyond the evidence. The strong sources (vanDussen's BRRP study; the DNB) find no Wycliffite conversion and identify the real conduit as Czech students at Oxford (Faulfis, Knehnic), not the queen. Because the overreach traces directly to the Foxe/Wylie 'Wycliffite queen' tradition rather than to a fabrication by White, anachronistic is the correct bin and cannot be raised to supported.

impact.reasoning: Anne of Bohemia genuinely was a Bohemian princess and queen of England who valued vernacular Scripture, but the claim that she was a 'convert to Wycliffe's teachings' who 'widely circulated' his works in Bohemia overstates her role; modern scholarship (vanDussen) identifies Czech Oxford students, not the queen, as the real conduit. The claim supports the chapter's Wycliffe-to-Bohemia transmission narrative (centrality 3) but is redundant: Jerome's own documented trip to England and return with Wycliffe's writings (GC-06-024, supported) already establishes that transmission channel, so the argument does not depend on Anne's supposed role. Anachronistic rather than invented -- it reproduces the Foxe/Wylie 'Wycliffite queen' tradition that White inherited.

claims.quote: These works Huss read with interest; he believed their author to be a sincere Christian, and was inclined to regard with favor the reforms which he advocated.
claims.claim_text: Huss read Wycliffe's works with interest, believed Wycliffe to be a sincere Christian, and was inclined to favor the reforms Wycliffe advocated.

verification.rationale: Hus read and was strongly influenced by Wycliffe's writings, admired and defended many of his views, and copied/taught his works.

claims.quote: About this time there arrived in Prague two strangers from England, men of learning, who had received the light, and had come to spread it in this distant land.
claims.claim_text: Two English scholars arrived in Prague to spread Wycliffite teachings.

verification.rationale: English Wycliffites had contact with Prague, but the specific episode of two learned Englishmen arriving to propagate the doctrine (and later drawing pictures) is transmitted via Wylie and weakly attested; the standard accounts of Wycliffe's diffusion to Bohemia emphasize Czech students and Hus's own circle rather than this episode. Inconclusive.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – Wycliffe's diffusion via Czech students
Bethlehem Chapel (Wikipedia) – antithesis wall paintings from Hus's time (two-Englishmen episode not recorded)

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure: no attestation surfaced anywhere in the English, Czech, or German record for two English visitors arriving in Prague to spread Wycliffism; the episode traces to the same Comenius (1648) martyrological line feeding this whole cluster. The weakened version - Wycliffite teaching reaching Prague via people traveling from England - is well-supported through entirely different, documented channels (Jerome of Prague, Faulfis and Knehnic, Wyche, Payne), so the failure is retrojection of a plausible general phenomenon onto invented specific actors. As written this is the founding premise of a five-paragraph invented vignette (027-032); centrality is moderate rather than load-bearing because the chapter already establishes Huss's turn toward Wycliffe independently in paragraph 6 (Jerome's imported writings, Queen Anne's circulation), so deleting the whole vignette leaves that causal claim intact through its other supports.

claims.quote: Beginning with an open attack on the pope’s supremacy, they were soon silenced by the authorities; but being unwilling to relinquish their purpose, they had recourse to other measures.
claims.claim_text: The two English strangers in Prague began with an open attack on the pope's supremacy, were silenced by the authorities, and resorted to other means to spread their message.

verification.rationale: Part of the same weakly attested 'two Englishmen' episode from Wylie; not independently corroborated in the standard accounts. Inconclusive.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure dependent on the same unattested episode as GC-06-027: the specific disputation-and-suppression scene involving the two strangers has no record, though the weakened, depersonalized claim (Wycliffite challenges to papal authority in Prague were suppressed, advocacy turning to indirect means) is well-supported by independently documented events (the 1403 condemnation of Wyclif's articles, the 1408 Black Rose disputation, 1410 book-burnings) - a rhetoric/absoluteness failure attaching a real institutional pattern to fictional actors. Centrality is moderate, tied to the same connective vignette as 027; it adds texture to the chapter's account of Bohemian resistance but nothing in the chapter's own trial/martyrdom argument depends on it.

claims.quote: In a place open to the public they drew two pictures. One represented the entrance of Christ into Jerusalem, “meek, and sitting upon an ass,”(127) and followed by His disciples in travel-worn garments and with naked feet. The other picture portrayed a pontifical procession,—the pope arrayed in his rich robes and triple crown, mounted upon a horse magnificently adorned, preceded by trumpeters, and followed by cardinals and prelates in dazzling array.
claims.claim_text: The two English strangers publicly displayed two contrasting pictures in Prague: Christ entering Jerusalem meekly on an ass with travel-worn disciples, and the pope in a pontifical procession in rich robes and triple crown on an adorned horse, preceded by trumpeters and followed by cardinals and prelates.

verification.rationale: The humble-Christ vs pompous-pope antithesis is a genuine, widespread Hussite motif (the Bethlehem Chapel wall paintings from Hus's time contrast Christ's poverty with Rome's riches; later famous via Cranach's Passional 1521). But the specific claim that two English strangers drew these pictures in Prague traces to Wylie and is not independently confirmed. The imagery is real; the two-Englishmen framing is not documented.

sources.title: Bethlehem Chapel (Wikipedia) – antithesis wall paintings contrasting Christ's poverty with Rome's riches

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure with an unusual twist: the act of these two named strangers publicly displaying the pictures is unattested, but the pictorial content itself - Christ meek on an ass versus the pope in triple crown on a mounted procession - is a genuinely documented Hussite propaganda motif (Nicholas of Dresden's Tabule, the Bethlehem Chapel murals Hus himself referenced, the Jena Codex), only misattributed roughly a decade early and to the wrong actors, so the weakened version reaches well-supported. Centrality is real rather than merely decorative: this motif recurs later in the chapter (paragraph 29, Huss's own documented prison dream about the Bethlehem Christ-pictures), giving it more narrative weight than a throwaway detail, though the chapter's argument about Huss's martyrdom does not depend on when or by whom the antithesis imagery first appeared.

claims.quote: Here was a sermon which arrested the attention of all classes. Crowds came to gaze upon the drawings.
claims.claim_text: The two pictures displayed in Prague drew crowds and attracted attention across social classes.

verification.rationale: Same weakly attested picture episode from Wylie; not independently corroborated. Inconclusive.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure and the weakest evidentiary link among the picture-episode claims: the audience for an undocumented display is, as the ruling notes, 'a fortiori undocumented,' and the 'all classes' universal has no independent basis of its own. The weakened, generalized claim about broad social reach for Hussite antithesis propaganda reaches only probable, resting on inference from separate evidence (Nicholas of Dresden's Tables, the 1408 disputation crowd). Centrality is low: crowd reaction is a minor embellishment within an already non-central vignette, and deleting it affects nothing in the chapter's substantive argument.

claims.quote: There was great commotion in Prague, and the strangers after a time found it necessary, for their own safety, to depart.
claims.claim_text: The display of the contrasting pictures caused great commotion in Prague, forcing the two English strangers to depart for their safety.

verification.rationale: Same weakly attested episode from Wylie; not independently corroborated. Inconclusive.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure that is the most complete failure in this cluster: unlike its neighbors, no weakened version survives at all (ruling_weakened: n/a) - even a generalized 'the display provoked commotion and endangered its makers' claim has no independent correlate, since the one comparable documented case (Nicholas of Dresden's later exile and reported burning) belongs to different, later, named persons and cannot rehabilitate this scene. This is pure narrative closure for an already-fabricated legend, with nothing left standing even at a softened reading. Centrality remains low despite the total evidentiary failure, because the scene only closes out a vignette that is itself dispensable to the chapter's argument about Huss and Jerome's documented fate.

claims.quote: The pictures made a deep impression on the mind of Huss, and led him to a closer study of the Bible and of Wycliffe’s writings.
claims.claim_text: The contrasting pictures made a deep impression on Huss and led him to study the Bible and Wycliffe's writings more closely.

verification.rationale: Depends on the weakly attested picture episode; the causal effect on Hus is asserted by Wylie but not independently documented (though Hus did use antithesis imagery in the Bethlehem Chapel). Inconclusive.

sources.title: Bethlehem Chapel (Wikipedia) – Hus's own antithesis wall paintings

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: The failing components are tied between fact and frame (prioritized as fact per the charter), but the frame failure is substantively the important one: the claim that the pictures caused Huss's 'closer study of the Bible and of Wycliffe's writings' assigns a fictional cause to a real effect, since Huss's documented engagement with Wyclif (manuscript copies from 1398) predates the legend's 1404 setting and is better explained by university study and Jerome's imports. The weakened version reaches only probable - Huss genuinely valued and later deployed Christ/pope imagery at Bethlehem Chapel, attested in his own Constance prison letter - but that supports influence running the other direction, not this specific causal origin story. Centrality is the highest in this cluster: this is the hinge claim connecting the invented picture-vignette to Huss's real documented reform trajectory, most directly touching the continuity-of-truth-transmission thesis; it is still not load-bearing, because paragraph 6 already establishes Huss's independent turn toward Wycliffe's works through Jerome and Queen Anne, so the chapter's argument survives this claim's removal.

claims.quote: From Bohemia the light extended to Germany; for disturbances in the University of Prague caused the withdrawal of hundreds of German students.
claims.claim_text: Disturbances at the University of Prague caused the withdrawal of hundreds of German students.

verification.rationale: The Decree of Kutna Hora (Jan 1409) shifted university control to the Bohemian nation, prompting a large exodus of German masters and students (many hundreds/thousands), some of whom helped found Leipzig. 'Hundreds' is conservative.

claims.quote: Many of them had received from Huss their first knowledge of the Bible, and on their return they spread the gospel in their fatherland.
claims.claim_text: Many of the German students who left Prague had received their first knowledge of the Bible from Huss and spread the gospel in Germany upon their return.

verification.rationale: The Germans who left Prague in 1409 were overwhelmingly OPPONENTS of the Czech/Wycliffite party led by Hus; they departed because they lost their voting majority and rejected Wycliffe. Wylie's claim that they carried Hus's gospel home reverses the alignment.
verification.modern_consensus: The emigrating Germans were anti-Wycliffite adversaries, not disciples; their departure isolated Prague and did not spread Hus's gospel in Germany.
verification.period_historiography: Wylie framed the exodus as light spreading from Bohemia to Germany through Hus's students.

sources.title: The Kuttenberg Decree and the Withdrawal of the German Students (Brill)

defense.argument: The broad umbrella sentence ('From Bohemia the light extended to Germany; for disturbances in the University of Prague caused the withdrawal of hundreds of German students') is itself accurate: the Decree of Kutna Hora (1409) did trigger a large German exodus that dispersed Prague's academic community and helped found the University of Leipzig, spreading Prague's intellectual culture into the Empire. The steelman would read 'the light extended to Germany' loosely as this diffusion of learning and controversy. But the specific causal claim in this item — that the departing Germans 'had received from Huss their first knowledge of the Bible' and 'spread the gospel in their fatherland' — inverts the actual alignment. The emigrants were overwhelmingly the anti-Wycliffite, anti-Hus party; they left precisely BECAUSE the decree stripped them of their voting majority and they rejected Wycliffe, not because they were Hus's Bible-students. The Brill study and the Kutna Hora record establish this reversal firmly, so the item borders on outright contradiction. It stays in the anachronistic category only because the inverted framing traces to Wylie; the defense cannot make it supported or disputed.

impact.reasoning: This is the highest-severity confirmed item in the chapter: the departing German masters and students of 1409 were overwhelmingly the anti-Wycliffite, anti-Hus faction who left precisely because the Decree of Kutna Hora stripped them of their voting majority, not disciples who had 'received from Huss their first knowledge of the Bible.' The defender itself flags that this borders on outright contradiction of the documented alignment, which justifies severity 4 over the other anachronistic items where the core event is merely embellished rather than reversed. Centrality is high because the sentence is the chapter's explicit bridge claiming Bohemian light 'extended to Germany,' foreshadowing the German Reformation the central thesis chain moves toward next. It is not load-bearing, though: the sentence is a single aside not developed further in the chapter, and the broader German Reformation narrative is carried independently in chapter 7 without reference to this exodus. As with the other anachronistic items, the reversal traces to Wylie's framing rather than to White inventing an event.

claims.quote: Tidings of the work at Prague were carried to Rome, and Huss was soon summoned to appear before the pope.
claims.claim_text: News of Huss's activities at Prague reached Rome, and Huss was summoned to appear before the pope.

verification.rationale: Complaints reached the Curia and Hus was summoned to appear personally (Rome) c.1410; he did not go and was excommunicated.

claims.quote: The king and queen of Bohemia, the university, members of the nobility, and officers of the government, united in an appeal to the pontiff that Huss be permitted to remain at Prague, and to answer at Rome by deputy.
claims.claim_text: The king and queen of Bohemia, the university, nobility, and government officials jointly appealed to the pope to allow Huss to remain in Prague and answer by deputy.

verification.rationale: Wenceslaus IV, Queen Sophia, the university and nobles interceded for Hus and sought to have him excused from personal appearance, answering by proctors.

claims.quote: Instead of granting this request, the pope proceeded to the trial and condemnation of Huss, and then declared the city of Prague to be under interdict.
claims.claim_text: The pope denied the Bohemian appeal, proceeded to try and condemn Huss, and declared Prague under interdict.

verification.rationale: Hus was excommunicated at the Curia and a papal interdict was laid on Prague (1412) while he remained there.

claims.quote: It was believed that the gates of heaven were closed against the region smitten with interdict; that until it should please the pope to remove the ban, the dead were shut out from the abodes of bliss.
claims.claim_text: It was popularly believed in that era that an interdict closed the gates of heaven against the afflicted region and shut the dead out from the abodes of bliss until the pope removed the ban.
claims.extractor_note: Describes a period belief/practice regarding papal interdicts; treated as a checkable historical-practice claim rather than theological assertion by the author.

verification.rationale: A medieval interdict suspended sacraments and Christian burial, and popular belief dreaded the consequences for the dead denied rites. GC dramatizes ('gates of heaven closed') but reflects the real terror an interdict inspired.

claims.quote: In token of this terrible calamity, all the services of religion were suspended. The churches were closed. Marriages were solemnized in the churchyard. The dead, denied burial in consecrated ground, were interred, without the rites of sepulture, in the ditches or the fields.
claims.claim_text: Under an interdict, religious services were suspended, churches were closed, marriages were performed in churchyards, and the dead were buried without rites in ditches or fields rather than consecrated ground.

verification.rationale: Accurate general effects of a medieval interdict: suspended worship, closed churches, denial of church burial, marriages without the Mass.

sources.title: Council of Constance / interdict practice (Catholic Encyclopedia)

claims.quote: The city of Prague was filled with tumult. A large class denounced Huss as the cause of all their calamities, and demanded that he be given up to the vengeance of Rome.
claims.claim_text: Prague was filled with tumult after the interdict, with a large class denouncing Huss and demanding he be surrendered to Rome.

verification.rationale: The interdict caused turmoil in Prague and a faction blamed Hus for the city's spiritual deprivation, contributing to his withdrawal.

claims.quote: To quiet the storm, the Reformer withdrew for a time to his native village.
claims.claim_text: To quiet the unrest, Huss withdrew for a time to his native village.

verification.rationale: Hus left Prague in late 1412 for the countryside (Kozi Hradek, later Krakovec) to relieve the interdict, continuing to preach and write.

claims.quote: Writing to the friends whom he had left at Prague, he said: “If I have withdrawn from the midst of you, it is to follow the precept and example of Jesus Christ, in order not to give room to the ill-minded to draw on themselves eternal condemnation, and in order not to be to the pious a cause of affliction and persecution, I have retired also through an apprehension that impious priests might continue for a longer time to prohibit the preaching of the word of God amongst you; but I have not quitted you to deny the divine truth, for which, with God’s assistance, I am willing to die.”(128)
claims.claim_text: Huss wrote to his friends at Prague explaining that his withdrawal followed Christ's example and was not a denial of the divine truth for which he was willing to die.

verification.rationale: From Hus's authentic correspondence (GC cites Bonnechose); 82 letters survive (largely through Mladonovice). Reflects his stated rationale for leaving Prague.

sources.title: The Letters of John Hus – Introduction (Wikisource)

claims.quote: Huss did not cease his labors, but traveled through the surrounding country, preaching to eager crowds.
claims.claim_text: While withdrawn from Prague, Huss continued preaching to eager crowds through the surrounding countryside.

verification.rationale: During rural exile Hus preached widely and wrote major works (incl. De Ecclesia); his preaching drew crowds.

claims.quote: “We can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth.”(129)
claims.claim_text: A quotation attributed in this account (source 129) states "We can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth," applied to Huss's continued spread of the gospel.

verification.rationale: Direct quotation of 2 Corinthians 13:8, accurately rendered.

claims.quote: “The mind of Huss, at this stage of his career, would seem to have been the scene of a painful conflict. Although the church was seeking to overwhelm him by her thunderbolts, he had not renounced her authority. The Roman Church was still to him the spouse of Christ, and the pope was the representative and vicar of God.”(130)
claims.claim_text: A quoted historical source (reference 130) asserts that at this stage Huss had not renounced the authority of the Roman Church, which he still regarded as the spouse of Christ, and still viewed the pope as God's representative and vicar.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the consensus that Hus remained a moderate who never fully repudiated the church or papal office, unlike later reformers.

claims.quote: This led him to adopt for his own guidance, and to preach to others for theirs, the maxim that the precepts of Scripture, conveyed through the understanding, are to rule the conscience; in other words, that God speaking in the Bible, and not the church speaking through the priesthood, is the one infallible guide.
claims.claim_text: The same quoted source states that Huss adopted and preached the maxim that Scripture, not the church speaking through the priesthood, is the one infallible guide for conscience.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the consensus that Hus grounded authority in Scripture over priestly authority, a hallmark of his Wycliffe-influenced teaching.

claims.quote: When after a time the excitement in Prague subsided, Huss returned to his chapel of Bethlehem, to continue with greater zeal and courage the preaching of the word of God.
claims.claim_text: After unrest in Prague subsided, Huss returned to the Bethlehem chapel to continue preaching with greater zeal and courage.

verification.rationale: Hus returned to Prague and Bethlehem Chapel preaching between periods of rural exile before departing for Constance in 1414.

claims.quote: His enemies were active and powerful, but the queen and many of the nobles were his friends, and the people in great numbers sided with him.
claims.claim_text: The queen and many nobles were friends of Huss, and large numbers of the people sided with him, despite active and powerful enemies.

verification.rationale: Queen Sophia favored Hus, and he had strong support among Bohemian nobility and the Czech populace despite clerical/German opposition.

claims.quote: Hitherto Huss had stood alone in his labors; but now Jerome, who while in England had accepted the teachings of Wycliffe, joined in the work of reform.
claims.claim_text: Jerome of Prague, who had accepted Wycliffe's teachings while in England, joined Huss in the work of reform.

verification.rationale: Jerome absorbed Wycliffe at Oxford and became a leading associate of Hus. 'Stood alone' overstates Hus's isolation, but Jerome's alignment is accurate.

claims.quote: Brilliancy of genius, eloquence and learning—gifts that win popular favor—were possessed in a preeminent degree by Jerome; but in those qualities which constitute real strength of character, Huss was the greater.
claims.claim_text: Jerome possessed brilliance, eloquence, and learning in a preeminent degree, while Huss possessed greater strength of character.

verification.rationale: Comparative character judgment; not testable. (The smuggled element—Jerome's noted eloquence/learning—is well attested, e.g. Poggio's admiration, but the claim is evaluative.)

claims.quote: Under their united labors the reform was more rapidly extended.
claims.claim_text: The Bohemian reform movement spread more rapidly under the united labors of Huss and Jerome.

verification.rationale: Hus and Jerome were the two most prominent leaders and their combined activity advanced the movement; 'more rapidly' is a reasonable but unquantified inference.

claims.quote: God permitted great light to shine upon the minds of these chosen men, revealing to them many of the errors of Rome; but they did not receive all the light that was to be given to the world.
claims.claim_text: God permitted great light revealing many errors of Rome to shine on Huss and Jerome, though not all the light later given to the world.

verification.rationale: Theological claim about divine illumination; not testable.

claims.quote: The schism in the church still continued. Three popes were now contending for the supremacy, and their strife filled Christendom with crime and tumult.
claims.claim_text: During this period, three rival popes were contending for supremacy in a schism that filled Christendom with crime and tumult.

verification.rationale: After Pisa (1409) three claimants contended (Roman Gregory XII, Avignon Benedict XIII, Pisan Alexander V then John XXIII), prolonging the Western Schism.

claims.quote: Each cast about him to purchase arms and to obtain soldiers. Of course money must be had; and to procure this, the gifts, offices, and blessings of the church were offered for sale.(131)
claims.claim_text: Each of the three rival popes sought to purchase arms and soldiers, funding this by selling the gifts, offices, and blessings of the church.

verification.rationale: The schism popes waged armed struggles financed by widespread simony and sale of offices/indulgences; John XXIII was a militarized figure deposed for notorious simony.

claims.quote: The priests also, imitating their superiors, resorted to simony and war to humble their rivals and strengthen their own power.
claims.claim_text: Priests imitated their superiors during the papal schism by resorting to simony and war to strengthen their own power.

verification.rationale: Simony and clerical corruption were pervasive during the schism, a chief grievance Constance's reform agenda addressed.

claims.quote: With daily increasing boldness, Huss thundered against the abominations which were tolerated in the name of religion; and the people openly accused the Romish leaders as the cause of the miseries that overwhelmed Christendom.
claims.claim_text: Huss increasingly denounced the abuses tolerated in the name of religion, and the people openly blamed Romish leaders for Christendom's miseries.

verification.rationale: Hus grew outspoken against corruption and the sale of indulgences (notably the 1412 controversy under John XXIII), amid high anticlerical feeling.

claims.quote: Again the city of Prague seemed on the verge of a bloody conflict. As in former ages, God’s servant was accused as “he that troubleth Israel.”(132) The city was again placed under interdict, and Huss withdrew to his native village.
claims.claim_text: Prague again neared bloody conflict, was again placed under interdict, and Huss again withdrew to his native village.

verification.rationale: The 1412 indulgence crisis brought renewed turmoil and a strengthened interdict, leading to Hus's renewed withdrawal. ('He that troubleth Israel' = 1 Kings 18:17.)

claims.quote: To cure the evils that were distracting Europe, a general council was summoned to meet at Constance.
claims.claim_text: A general church council was summoned to meet at Constance to address the evils distracting Europe.

verification.rationale: The Council of Constance (1414–1418) was convened to end the schism, combat heresy, and reform the church.

claims.quote: The council was called at the desire of the emperor Sigismund, by one of the three rival popes, John XXIII.
claims.claim_text: The Council of Constance was called at the desire of Emperor Sigismund by Pope John XXIII, one of the three rival popes.

verification.rationale: Under pressure from Sigismund, the Pisan pope John XXIII formally convoked Constance in 1414.

claims.quote: The demand for a council had been far from welcome to Pope John, whose character and policy could ill bear investigation, even by prelates as lax in morals as were the churchmen of those times.
claims.claim_text: The demand for a council was unwelcome to Pope John XXIII because his character and policy could not withstand investigation.

verification.rationale: Interpretive claim about John XXIII's motives; not directly testable, though consistent with his later deposition.

claims.quote: He dared not, however, oppose the will of Sigismund.(133)
claims.claim_text: Pope John XXIII did not dare to oppose Emperor Sigismund's will to convene the council.

verification.rationale: John XXIII, needing Sigismund's protection, yielded to the emperor and convoked the council despite the risk.

claims.quote: The chief objects to be accomplished by the council were to heal the schism in the church, and to root out heresy.
claims.claim_text: The chief objects of the Council of Constance were to heal the papal schism and root out heresy.

verification.rationale: The council's aims were causa unionis (end schism), causa fidei (suppress heresy), and causa reformationis (reform); GC names the first two.

claims.quote: Hence the two anti-popes were summoned to appear before it, as well as the leading propagator of the new opinions, John Huss. The former, having regard to their own safety, did not attend in person, but were represented by their delegates.
claims.claim_text: The two anti-popes were summoned to the Council of Constance along with John Huss, but the anti-popes did not attend in person and were represented by delegates instead.

verification.rationale: Gregory XII and Benedict XIII acted through representatives, not in person; Hus came in person. 'Two anti-popes' reflects a later Roman perspective.

claims.quote: Pope John, while ostensibly the convoker of the council, came to it with many misgivings, suspecting the emperor’s secret purpose to depose him, and fearing to be brought to account for the vices which had disgraced the tiara, as well as for the crimes which had secured it.
claims.claim_text: Pope John XXIII came to the Council of Constance with misgivings, suspecting Sigismund intended to depose him and fearing accountability for his vices and crimes.

verification.rationale: John XXIII feared deposition, later fled in disguise, and was deposed and tried for simony/immorality, consistent with GC's account.

claims.quote: Yet he made his entry into the city of Constance with great pomp, attended by ecclesiastics of the highest rank, and followed by a train of courtiers. All the clergy and dignitaries of the city, with an immense crowd of citizens, went out to welcome him. Above his head was a golden canopy, borne by four of the chief magistrates. The host was carried before him, and the rich dresses of the cardinals and nobles made an imposing display.
claims.claim_text: Pope John XXIII entered Constance with great pomp, welcomed by clergy, dignitaries and crowds, under a golden canopy borne by four chief magistrates, with the host carried before him amid richly dressed cardinals and nobles.

verification.rationale: John XXIII's ceremonial entry into Constance (Oct 1414) was famously pompous; the pageantry details derive from Wylie/contemporary reports and are consistent with the record.

claims.quote: Huss was conscious of the dangers which threatened him. He parted from his friends as if he were never to meet them again, and went on his journey feeling that it was leading him to the stake.
claims.claim_text: Huss, aware of the dangers awaiting him, parted from his friends as if never to meet them again before journeying to Constance, feeling it led him to the stake.

verification.rationale: Hus's own pre-departure and prison letters show he anticipated the danger of death at Constance.

claims.quote: Notwithstanding he had obtained a safe-conduct from the king of Bohemia, and received one also from the emperor Sigismund while on his journey, he made all his arrangements in view of the probability of his death.
claims.claim_text: Huss obtained a safe-conduct from the king of Bohemia and another from Emperor Sigismund en route, yet made arrangements anticipating his probable death.

verification.rationale: Hus travelled under an imperial safe-conduct from Sigismund (delivered on the journey/shortly after arrival, 5 Nov 1414); he nevertheless prepared for likely death.

claims.quote: In a letter addressed to his friends at Prague he said: “My brethren, ... I am departing with a safe-conduct from the king, to meet my numerous and mortal enemies.... I confide altogether in the all-powerful God, in my Saviour; I trust that He will listen to your ardent prayers, that He will infuse His prudence and His wisdom into my mouth, in order that I may resist them; and that He will accord me His Holy Spirit to fortify me in His truth, so that I may face with courage, temptations, prison, and if necessary, a cruel death.”(134)
claims.claim_text: Huss wrote a letter to his friends at Prague stating he departed with a royal safe-conduct to meet his mortal enemies, trusting in God to sustain him through temptations, prison, and possibly a cruel death.

verification.rationale: From Hus's authentic correspondence (GC cites Bonnechose); farewell letters anticipating imprisonment and death before Constance are among the preserved letters.

claims.quote: Probably, therefore, you will never more behold my face at Prague: but should the will of the all-powerful God deign to restore me to you, let us then advance with a firmer heart in the knowledge and the love of His law.”
claims.claim_text: In the same letter, Huss told his Prague friends they would probably never see his face again, unless God's will restored him to them.

verification.rationale: Continuation of the same authentic Hus farewell letter (Bonnechose).

claims.quote: In another letter, to a priest who had become a disciple of the gospel, Huss spoke with deep humility of his own errors, accusing himself “of having felt pleasure in wearing rich apparel, and of having wasted hours in frivolous occupations.”
claims.claim_text: In a letter to a priest disciple, Huss confessed his own faults, including pleasure in rich apparel and wasted hours in frivolous occupations.

verification.rationale: From Hus's authentic letters (Bonnechose); self-critical confessions of earlier vanity appear in his prison correspondence.

claims.quote: “May the glory of God and the salvation of souls occupy thy mind, and not the possession of benefices and estates. Beware of adorning thy house more than thy soul; and above all, give thy care to the spiritual edifice. Be pious and humble with the poor, and consume not thy substance in feasting.”
claims.claim_text: In the same letter, Huss admonished the priest to prioritize God's glory and souls over benefices, estates, and feasting, and to be humble with the poor.

verification.rationale: Continuation of the authentic Hus letter to a young priest (Bonnechose); such pastoral admonitions are characteristic of his correspondence.

claims.quote: On the cover of the letter he added, “I conjure thee, my friend, not to break this seal until thou shalt have acquired the certitude that I am dead.”(135)
claims.claim_text: Huss added a note on the cover of his letter to the priest instructing him not to break the seal until certain of Huss's death.

verification.rationale: From Hus's authentic correspondence (Bonnechose); the instruction not to open the letter until his death is part of the preserved prison letters.

claims.quote: On his journey, Huss everywhere beheld indications of the spread of his doctrines, and the favor with which his cause was regarded. The people thronged to meet him, and in some towns the magistrates attended him through their streets.
claims.claim_text: On his journey to Constance, Huss saw widespread evidence of the spread of his doctrines; people thronged to meet him and magistrates in some towns escorted him through their streets.

verification.rationale: Hus reported in his own letters a favorable reception in German towns on the way to Constance, contrary to his expectations.

claims.quote: Upon arriving at Constance, Huss was granted full liberty. To the emperor’s safe-conduct was added a personal assurance of protection by the pope.
claims.claim_text: Upon arriving at Constance, Huss was granted full liberty, and the emperor's safe-conduct was supplemented by the pope's personal assurance of protection.

verification.rationale: Hus was initially at liberty for several weeks; the standard accounts (and Bonnechose) report that John XXIII gave him assurances of safety before his arrest on 28 Nov 1414.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – initial liberty then arrest

claims.quote: But in violation of these solemn and repeated declarations, the Reformer was in a short time arrested, by order of the pope and cardinals, and thrust into a loathsome dungeon.
claims.claim_text: In violation of the safe-conduct and papal assurances, Huss was soon arrested by order of the pope and cardinals and thrown into a dungeon.

verification.rationale: Hus was arrested on 28 Nov 1414 and imprisoned (initially in a Dominican cell by the Rhine), despite the safe-conduct.

claims.quote: Later he was transferred to a strong castle across the Rhine, and there kept a prisoner.
claims.claim_text: Huss was later transferred to a strong castle across the Rhine and kept prisoner there.

verification.rationale: Hus was moved to Gottlieben castle on the Rhine, held in harsh confinement.

claims.quote: The pope, profiting little by his perfidy, was soon after committed to the same prison.(136) He had been proved before the council to be guilty of the basest crimes, besides murder, simony, and adultery, “sins not fit to be named.” So the council itself declared; and he was finally deprived of the tiara, and thrown into prison.
claims.claim_text: Pope John XXIII was soon after committed to the same prison as Huss, having been proved before the council guilty of the basest crimes including murder, simony, and adultery, and was deprived of the tiara and imprisoned.

verification.rationale: John XXIII was deposed 29 May 1415 for simony/schism/scandalous life (with allegations of murder and immorality) and imprisoned; he was for a time held at Gottlieben, where Hus had been confined.

claims.quote: The anti-popes also were deposed, and a new pontiff was chosen.
claims.claim_text: The two anti-popes were deposed and a new pontiff was chosen by the Council of Constance.

verification.rationale: Gregory XII resigned (July 1415) and Benedict XIII was deposed; Martin V was elected in 1417, ending the schism.

claims.quote: Though the pope himself had been guilty of greater crimes than Huss had ever charged upon the priests, and for which he had demanded a reformation, yet the same council which degraded the pontiff proceeded to crush the Reformer.
claims.claim_text: The same Council of Constance that degraded the guilty pope proceeded to move against Huss, even though the pope's proven crimes exceeded those Huss had charged against corrupt priests.

verification.rationale: Control-sample item: the checkable core holds. The same council deposed John XXIII for grave proven crimes and then condemned and executed Hus; the juxtaposition is historically accurate.

claims.quote: The imprisonment of Huss excited great indignation in Bohemia. Powerful noblemen addressed to the council earnest protests against this outrage.
claims.claim_text: Huss's imprisonment provoked great indignation in Bohemia, prompting powerful noblemen to send earnest protests to the council.

verification.rationale: Bohemian and Moravian nobles protested Hus's imprisonment and (after his death) his execution; the famous protest of hundreds of nobles followed in Sept 1415.

claims.quote: The emperor, who was loath to permit the violation of a safe-conduct, opposed the proceedings against him.
claims.claim_text: Emperor Sigismund initially opposed the proceedings against Huss, being reluctant to permit violation of the safe-conduct.

verification.rationale: Sigismund was initially angered by the arrest as a breach of his safe-conduct, but by 1 Jan 1415 acquiesced and let the council proceed.

claims.quote: They brought forward arguments of great length to prove that “faith ought not to be kept with heretics, nor persons suspected of heresy, though they are furnished with safe-conducts from the emperor and kings.”(137) Thus they prevailed.
claims.claim_text: Huss's enemies at Constance argued at length that faith need not be kept with heretics even when furnished with safe-conducts from emperors and kings, and this argument prevailed on Sigismund.

verification.rationale: The council held the safe-conduct did not bar Hus's trial; the principle that faith need not be kept with a heretic was pressed on Sigismund, who yielded. GC's quotation (cited to Wylie) captures the argument made, though the council's precise juridical stance was debated.

claims.quote: Enfeebled by illness and imprisonment,—for the damp, foul air of his dungeon had brought on a fever which nearly ended his life,—Huss was at last brought before the council.
claims.claim_text: The damp, foul air of Huss's dungeon brought on a fever that nearly killed him before he was finally brought before the council.

verification.rationale: Hus fell gravely ill in harsh imprisonment; a fever that nearly killed him is reported in the standard accounts.

claims.quote: Loaded with chains, he stood in the presence of the emperor, whose honor and good faith had been pledged to protect him.
claims.claim_text: Huss stood in chains before Emperor Sigismund, whose honor and good faith had been pledged to protect him.

verification.rationale: Hus appeared before the council in Sigismund's presence; his public hearings took place with the emperor present.

claims.quote: During his long trial he firmly maintained the truth, and in the presence of the assembled dignitaries of church and state, he uttered a solemn and faithful protest against the corruptions of the hierarchy.
claims.claim_text: During his trial before the assembled dignitaries of church and state, Huss firmly maintained his positions and protested against the corruptions of the hierarchy.

verification.rationale: At his public hearings (5, 7, 8 June 1415) Hus defended his views and refused to recant, per Peter of Mladonovice's contemporary account.

claims.quote: When required to choose whether he would recant his doctrines or suffer death, he accepted the martyr’s fate.
claims.claim_text: When required to recant his doctrines or suffer death, Huss chose to accept martyrdom.

verification.rationale: Hus repeatedly refused to recant unless convinced from Scripture and accepted death; he was burned on 6 July 1415.

claims.quote: “I write this letter,” he said to a friend, “in my prison, and with my fettered hand, expecting my sentence of death to-morrow.... When, with the assistance of Jesus Christ, we shall again meet in the delicious peace of the future life, you will learn how merciful God has shown Himself toward me, how effectually He has supported me in the midst of my temptations and trials.”(138)
claims.claim_text: Huss wrote to a friend from prison, expecting his death sentence the next day, expressing that God had mercifully supported him through his trials.

verification.rationale: From Hus's authentic prison letters (Bonnechose); farewell letters written in chains expecting death are among the preserved correspondence.

claims.quote: Returning in his dreams to the chapel at Prague where he had preached the gospel, he saw the pope and his bishops effacing the pictures of Christ which he had painted on its walls.
claims.claim_text: While imprisoned, Huss reported a dream in which he returned to the Bethlehem chapel at Prague and saw the pope and bishops effacing pictures of Christ he had painted there.

verification.rationale: Hus related this dream in his own prison letters, which GC reproduces via Bonnechose.

claims.quote: “This vision distressed him: but on the next day he saw many painters occupied in restoring these figures in greater number and in brighter colors. As soon as their task was ended, the painters, who were surrounded by an immense crowd, exclaimed, ‘Now let the popes and bishops come; they shall never efface them more!’ ”
claims.claim_text: In Huss's reported dream, after the pictures were effaced he saw painters restoring them in greater number and brighter colors, and the painters, surrounded by a crowd, declared that popes and bishops would never efface them again.

verification.rationale: Continuation of Hus's own account of the dream in his prison letters (Bonnechose).

claims.quote: Said the Reformer, as he related his dream, “I maintain this for certain, that the image of Christ will never be effaced. They have wished to destroy it, but it shall be painted afresh in all hearts by much better preachers than myself.”(139)
claims.claim_text: Huss is quoted as saying, in relating his dream, that the image of Christ would never be effaced and would be painted afresh in all hearts by better preachers than himself.

verification.rationale: Hus's own interpretation of the dream in his prison correspondence (Bonnechose); parallels the hope for better preachers to come expressed elsewhere in his letters.

claims.quote: For the last time, Huss was brought before the council. It was a vast and brilliant assembly,—the emperor, the princes of the empire, the royal deputies, the cardinals, bishops, and priests, and an immense crowd who had come as spectators of the events of the day.
claims.claim_text: Huss was brought for the last time before a vast assembly at Constance including the emperor, princes of the empire, royal deputies, cardinals, bishops, priests, and a large crowd of spectators.

verification.rationale: Hus's final condemnation took place 6 July 1415 in the cathedral before the assembled council, the emperor, and a great crowd.

claims.quote: Being called upon for his final decision, Huss declared his refusal to abjure, and fixing his penetrating glance upon the monarch whose plighted word had been so shamelessly violated, he declared, “I determined, of my own free will, to appear before this council, under the public protection and faith of the emperor here present.”(140)
claims.claim_text: When called for his final decision, Huss refused to abjure and stated before the assembly that he had appeared before the council of his own free will under the emperor's public protection and pledged faith.

verification.rationale: Recorded in the contemporary Relatio of Peter of Mladonovice (via Bonnechose); Hus pointedly invoked the emperor's violated safe-conduct at his condemnation.

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice, tr. Spinka), archive.org

claims.quote: A deep flush crimsoned the face of Sigismund as the eyes of all in the assembly turned upon him.
claims.claim_text: Emperor Sigismund's face turned deep red when the assembly's eyes turned to him after Huss's statement about the violated safe-conduct.

verification.rationale: The detail that Sigismund blushed when Hus recalled the safe-conduct is reported in the contemporary trial accounts (Mladonovice tradition).

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice), archive.org

claims.quote: Sentence having been pronounced, the ceremony of degradation began. The bishops clothed their prisoner in the sacerdotal habit, and as he took the priestly robe, he said, “Our Lord Jesus Christ was covered with a white robe, by way of insult, when Herod had Him conducted before Pilate.”(141)
claims.claim_text: After sentence, bishops began a ceremony of degradation by clothing Huss in a priestly robe, and Huss remarked that Christ was likewise covered with a white robe in insult before Pilate.

verification.rationale: The degradation ceremony and Hus's sayings are recorded in Mladonovice's Relatio (via Bonnechose).

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice), archive.org

claims.quote: Being again exhorted to retract, he replied, turning toward the people: “With what face, then, should I behold the heavens? How should I look on those multitudes of men to whom I have preached the pure gospel? No; I esteem their salvation more than this poor body, now appointed unto death.”
claims.claim_text: When again exhorted to retract, Huss replied to the people that he valued their salvation more than his own body appointed to death.

verification.rationale: Recorded in Mladonovice's contemporary account of the degradation (via Bonnechose).

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice), archive.org

claims.quote: The vestments were removed one by one, each bishop pronouncing a curse as he performed his part of the ceremony.
claims.claim_text: During the degradation ceremony, Huss's vestments were removed one by one, with each bishop pronouncing a curse.

verification.rationale: The formal degradation involved stripping the vestments one by one with maledictions, as described in the contemporary accounts.

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice), archive.org

claims.quote: Finally “they put on his head a cap or pyramidal-shaped mitre of paper, on which were painted frightful figures of demons, with the word ‘Arch-Heretic’ conspicuous in front.
claims.claim_text: As the final step of the degradation ceremony, a paper mitre painted with demon figures and the label "Arch-Heretic" was placed on Huss's head.

verification.rationale: A tall paper mitre painted with demons and inscribed 'Haeresiarcha' (leader of heretics) was placed on Hus's head, a well-attested detail.

claims.quote: ‘Most joyfully,’ said Huss, ‘will I wear this crown of shame for Thy sake, O Jesus, who for me didst wear a crown of thorns.’ ”
claims.claim_text: Huss is quoted as saying he would most joyfully wear the paper mitre as a crown of shame for Christ's sake.

verification.rationale: Hus's saying comparing the paper crown to Christ's crown of thorns is recorded in the contemporary Relatio (via Bonnechose).

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice), archive.org

claims.quote: When he was thus arrayed, “the prelates said, ‘Now we devote thy soul to the devil.’ ‘And I,’ said John Huss, lifting up his eyes toward heaven, ‘do commit my spirit into Thy hands, O Lord Jesus, for Thou hast redeemed me.’ ”(142)
claims.claim_text: After being arrayed in the mock mitre, the prelates declared they devoted Huss's soul to the devil, and Huss responded by committing his spirit into the hands of Jesus.

verification.rationale: The exchange ('we devote thy soul to the devil' / 'I commit my spirit') is recorded in the contemporary trial accounts (Mladonovice, via Bonnechose).

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice), archive.org

claims.quote: He was now delivered up to the secular authorities, and led away to the place of execution. An immense procession followed, hundreds of men at arms, priests and bishops in their costly robes, and the inhabitants of Constance.
claims.claim_text: Huss was delivered to secular authorities and led to execution accompanied by an immense procession including hundreds of armed men, priests and bishops in costly robes, and the inhabitants of Constance.

verification.rationale: After degradation Hus was handed to the secular arm and led out to be burned, accompanied by a large armed and clerical procession.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – handed to secular arm and executed

claims.quote: “What errors,” said Huss, “shall I renounce? I know myself guilty of none. I call God to witness that all that I have written and preached has been with the view of rescuing souls from sin and perdition; and, therefore, most joyfully will I confirm with my blood that truth which I have written and preached.”(143)
claims.claim_text: At the stake, when exhorted once more to renounce his errors, Huss replied that he knew himself guilty of none and would joyfully confirm with his blood the truth he had written and preached.

verification.rationale: Hus's final refusal to recant at the stake is recorded in the contemporary Relatio (via Bonnechose).

sources.title: John Hus at the Council of Constance (Mladonovice), archive.org

claims.quote: When the flames kindled about him, he began to sing, “Jesus, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me,” and so continued till his voice was silenced forever.
claims.claim_text: As the flames were kindled around him at the stake, Huss began singing "Jesus, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me" until his voice was silenced by death.

verification.rationale: Peter of Mladonovice, an eyewitness, records that Hus sang/prayed (Christe fili Dei... miserere) as the flames rose.

claims.quote: A zealous papist, describing the martyrdom of Huss, and of Jerome, who died soon after, said: “Both bore themselves with constant mind when their last hour approached. They prepared for the fire as if they were going to a marriage feast. They uttered no cry of pain. When the flames rose, they began to sing hymns; and scarce could the vehemency of the fire stop their singing.”(144)
claims.claim_text: An unnamed zealous papist is quoted describing that both Huss and Jerome bore themselves with constant mind at their deaths, prepared for the fire as if for a marriage feast, uttered no cry of pain, and sang hymns as the flames rose.

verification.rationale: The 'zealous papist' is Aeneas Sylvius Piccolomini (later Pope Pius II), whose Historia Bohemica famously describes Hus and Jerome going to death as to a banquet, singing amid the flames; GC cites this via Wylie.

sources.title: Jerome of Prague (Britannica) – contemporary Catholic admiration

claims.quote: and of Jerome, who died soon after
claims.claim_text: Jerome of Prague died soon after Huss's execution.

verification.rationale: Jerome was executed 30 May 1416, about ten and a half months after Hus (6 July 1415).

claims.quote: When the body of Huss had been wholly consumed, his ashes, with the soil upon which they rested, were gathered up and cast into the Rhine, and thus borne onward to the ocean.
claims.claim_text: After Huss's body was wholly consumed by fire, his ashes and the soil beneath were gathered and cast into the Rhine River, to be carried to the ocean.

verification.rationale: Hus's ashes (with surrounding earth) were thrown into the Rhine to prevent relic veneration, a well-attested detail.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – ashes thrown into the Rhine

claims.quote: Jerome, upon bidding farewell to Huss on his departure for the council, had exhorted him to courage and firmness, declaring that if he should fall into any peril, he himself would fly to his assistance.
claims.claim_text: When Huss departed for the Council of Constance, Jerome exhorted him to courage and promised to come to his aid if Huss fell into peril.

verification.rationale: Jerome did follow Hus to Constance to support him; the promise to come to his aid is part of the traditional account and consistent with his actions.
