claims.quote: Upon hearing of the Reformer’s imprisonment, the faithful disciple immediately prepared to fulfil his promise. Without a safe-conduct he set out, with a single companion, for Constance.
claims.claim_text: Upon hearing of Huss's imprisonment, Jerome set out for Constance without a safe-conduct, accompanied by a single companion.

verification.rationale: Jerome travelled to Constance (April 1415) to support Hus without effective safe-conduct.

claims.quote: On arriving there he was convinced that he had only exposed himself to peril, without the possibility of doing anything for the deliverance of Huss. He fled from the city, but was arrested on the homeward journey, and brought back loaded with fetters, and under the custody of a band of soldiers.
claims.claim_text: Jerome, realizing at Constance he could not help Huss, fled the city but was arrested on his homeward journey and brought back in fetters under armed guard.

verification.rationale: Jerome fled Constance, was arrested on the way home, and was brought back in chains under guard.

claims.quote: At his first appearance before the council, his attempts to reply to the accusations brought against him were met with shouts, “To the flames with him! to the flames!”(145)
claims.claim_text: At Jerome's first appearance before the Council of Constance, his attempts to answer accusations were met with shouts of "To the flames with him!"

verification.rationale: Jerome's first appearances were tumultuous, with hostile shouting; consistent with the contemporary and traditional accounts.

claims.quote: He was thrown into a dungeon, chained in a position which caused him great suffering, and fed on bread and water.
claims.claim_text: Jerome was thrown into a dungeon, chained in a position causing great suffering, and fed only bread and water.

verification.rationale: Jerome was held nearly a year in exceptionally harsh imprisonment (chained, poorly fed), as reported in the biographies.

claims.quote: After some months the cruelties of his imprisonment brought upon Jerome an illness that threatened his life, and his enemies, fearing that he might escape them, treated him with less severity, though he remained in prison for one year.
claims.claim_text: Jerome fell seriously ill after months of harsh imprisonment, leading his captors to treat him less severely, though he remained imprisoned for one year total.

verification.rationale: Jerome was imprisoned roughly a year (arrested ~May 1415, executed 30 May 1416) and fell ill under the harsh conditions.

claims.quote: The violation of his safe-conduct had roused a storm of indignation, and as the safer course, the council determined, instead of burning Jerome, to force him, if possible, to retract.
claims.claim_text: Because the violation of Huss's safe-conduct had roused indignation, the council decided to try to force Jerome to retract rather than burn him outright.

verification.rationale: The council pressed Jerome to recant (which he initially did), consistent with wanting to avoid another martyrdom after the outrage over Hus.

claims.quote: He was brought before the assembly, and offered the alternative to recant, or to die at the stake.
claims.claim_text: Jerome was brought before the council assembly and offered the choice of recanting or dying at the stake.

verification.rationale: Jerome was confronted with the choice to recant or be burned; he first recanted, then later retracted his recantation.

claims.quote: Jerome’s fortitude gave way, and he consented to submit to the council. He pledged himself to adhere to the Catholic faith, and accepted the action of the council in condemning the doctrines of Wycliffe and Huss, excepting, however, the “holy truths” which they had taught.(146)
claims.claim_text: Jerome's resolve gave way and he submitted to the council, pledging adherence to the Catholic faith and accepting the council's condemnation of Wycliffe's and Huss's doctrines, except for the "holy truths" they taught.

verification.rationale: Jerome recanted in Sept 1415, professing Catholic orthodoxy and assenting to the condemnation of Wycliffe and Hus.

sources.title: Jerome of Prague (Britannica) – recantation then withdrawal

claims.quote: But in the solitude of his dungeon he saw more clearly what he had done. He thought of the courage and fidelity of Huss, and in contrast pondered upon his own denial of the truth.
claims.claim_text: In the solitude of his dungeon after recanting, Jerome reflected on his own denial of the truth in contrast to Huss's courage and fidelity.

verification.rationale: Interpretive of Jerome's inner state, but grounded in the documented fact that he repented of his recantation and withdrew it.

claims.quote: His resolution was taken: to escape a brief period of suffering he would not deny his Lord.
claims.claim_text: Jerome resolved that he would not deny his Lord merely to escape a brief period of suffering.

verification.rationale: Interpretive but grounded in the fact that Jerome withdrew his recantation and chose death over renewed denial.

claims.quote: Soon he was again brought before the council. His submission had not satisfied his judges.
claims.claim_text: Jerome was again brought before the council because his prior submission had not satisfied his judges.

verification.rationale: Jerome's recantation was deemed insincere/ambiguous and he was brought again before the council (May 1416).

claims.quote: But he had determined to avow his faith, and follow his brother-martyr to the flames.
claims.claim_text: Jerome had determined to avow his faith and follow Huss to martyrdom by fire.

verification.rationale: At his final appearance Jerome withdrew his recantation and openly reaffirmed the teachings of Wycliffe and Hus, accepting death.

claims.quote: He renounced his former recantation, and as a dying man, solemnly required an opportunity to make his defense.
claims.claim_text: Jerome renounced his earlier recantation and requested, as a man expecting death, the opportunity to make his defense.

verification.rationale: Jerome publicly renounced his recantation and was granted a hearing before the council in May 1416.

claims.quote: “You have held me shut up three hundred and forty days in a frightful prison,” he said, “in the midst of filth, noisomeness, stench, and the utmost want of everything; you then bring me out before you, and lending an ear to my mortal enemies, you refuse to hear me.”(147)
claims.claim_text: Jerome stated to the council that he had been held 340 days in a frightful prison amid filth, stench, and want, and that the council refused to hear him while giving ear to his enemies.

verification.rationale: Jerome's complaint about ~340 days in a foul prison and being denied a hearing is recorded in the traditional accounts (Bonnechose); the figure is consistent with roughly a year's imprisonment.

claims.quote: His request was finally granted. In the presence of his judges, Jerome kneeled down and prayed that the divine Spirit might control his thoughts and words, that he might speak nothing contrary to the truth or unworthy of his Master.
claims.claim_text: Jerome's request to defend himself was granted, and he knelt and prayed before his judges that the divine Spirit would guide his words.

verification.rationale: Jerome was granted a public hearing (25–26 May 1416) at which he delivered a celebrated defense.

sources.title: Jerome of Prague (Britannica) – defense before the council

claims.quote: The words of Jerome excited astonishment and admiration, even in his enemies. For a whole year he had been immured in a dungeon, unable to read or even to see, in great physical suffering and mental anxiety. Yet his arguments were presented with as much clearness and power as if he had had undisturbed opportunity for study.
claims.claim_text: Jerome's defense speech astonished even his enemies, delivered with clarity and power despite a year immured in a dungeon unable to read or see, amid great physical suffering.

verification.rationale: Poggio Bracciolini, an eyewitness, marveled at Jerome's eloquence and learning delivered after long, harsh imprisonment.

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: He pointed his hearers to the long line of holy men who had been condemned by unjust judges.
claims.claim_text: In his defense, Jerome pointed to a long line of holy men historically condemned by unjust judges.

verification.rationale: Poggio's eyewitness letter records Jerome citing precedents of righteous men condemned by unjust tribunals.

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: Christ Himself was condemned as a malefactor at an unrighteous tribunal.
claims.claim_text: Jerome's defense invoked the fact that Christ himself was condemned as a malefactor at an unrighteous tribunal.

verification.rationale: Theological allusion; not an empirically checkable historical claim as framed here.

claims.quote: “I knew him from his childhood,” he said. “He was a most excellent man, just and holy; he was condemned, notwithstanding his innocence....
claims.claim_text: Jerome testified before the council that he had known Huss from childhood and that Huss was a most excellent, just, and holy man condemned despite his innocence.

verification.rationale: Jerome's praise of Hus as innocent and holy is recorded in the accounts of his final defense (Bonnechose; Poggio).

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: I also—I am ready to die: I will not recoil before the torments that are prepared for me by my enemies and false witnesses, who will one day have to render an account of their impostures before the great God, whom nothing can deceive.”(149)
claims.claim_text: Jerome declared himself ready to die and stated his enemies and false witnesses would answer to God for their impostures.

verification.rationale: Consistent with Jerome's defiant final defense as recorded by Poggio and transmitted via Bonnechose.

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: “Of all the sins that I have committed since my youth, none weigh so heavily on my mind, and cause me such poignant remorse, as that which I committed in this fatal place, when I approved of the iniquitous sentence rendered against Wycliffe, and against the holy martyr, John Huss, my master and my friend.”
claims.claim_text: Jerome declared that his greatest remorse was for having approved the sentence against Wycliffe and John Huss, whom he called his master and friend.

verification.rationale: Jerome's expression of remorse for his earlier recantation (approving the condemnation of Wycliffe and Hus) is a well-attested feature of his final speech.

claims.quote: “You condemned Wycliffe and John Huss, not for having shaken the doctrine of the church, but simply because they branded with reprobation the scandals proceeding from the clergy,—their pomp, their pride, and all the vices of the prelates and priests. The things which they have affirmed, and which are irrefutable, I also think and declare, like them.”
claims.claim_text: Jerome told his judges they had condemned Wycliffe and Huss not for doctrinal deviation but for denouncing clerical scandals, pomp, pride and vice, and that he affirmed the same irrefutable points as they did.

verification.rationale: Consistent with Jerome's recorded final defense (Poggio/Bonnechose), in which he openly reaffirmed the positions of Wycliffe and Hus.

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: The prelates, trembling with rage, cried out, “What need is there of further proof? We behold with our own eyes the most obstinate of heretics!”
claims.claim_text: The prelates, enraged at Jerome's defense, cried out that they saw with their own eyes the most obstinate of heretics and needed no further proof.

verification.rationale: The hostile reaction of the prelates to Jerome's open avowal is part of the traditional account (Bonnechose) of his final hearing.

claims.quote: “You have held me for a whole year in a frightful dungeon, more horrible than death itself. You have treated me more cruelly than a Turk, Jew, or pagan, and my flesh has literally rotted off my bones alive; and yet I make no complaint, for lamentation ill becomes a man of heart and spirit; but I cannot but express my astonishment at such great barbarity toward a Christian.”(150)
claims.claim_text: Jerome told the council he had been held a whole year in a dungeon more horrible than death, treated more cruelly than a Turk, Jew, or pagan, with his flesh rotting off his bones.

verification.rationale: Jerome's protest against his year-long, brutal imprisonment is recorded in the traditional accounts (Bonnechose), consistent with the documented harshness of his confinement.

claims.quote: Again the storm of rage burst out, and Jerome was hurried away to prison. Yet there were some in the assembly upon whom his words had made a deep impression, and who desired to save his life.
claims.claim_text: After the outburst of rage, Jerome was returned to prison, though some in the assembly, moved by his defense, wished to save his life.

verification.rationale: Poggio himself was among those impressed by Jerome; the account of some wishing to spare him is consistent with the eyewitness admiration.

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: He was visited by dignitaries of the church, and urged to submit himself to the council. The most brilliant prospects were presented before him as the reward of renouncing his opposition to Rome.
claims.claim_text: Church dignitaries visited Jerome in prison, urging him to submit to the council and offering brilliant prospects as reward for renouncing his opposition to Rome.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the account that Jerome was repeatedly pressed to recant with inducements before his execution.

claims.quote: “Prove to me from the Holy Writings that I am in error,” he said, “and I will abjure it.”
claims.claim_text: Jerome told his tempters that he would abjure his positions if they could prove from Scripture that he was in error.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the Wycliffite/Hussite appeal to Scripture as sole authority, echoed in the traditional accounts of Jerome (Bonnechose).

claims.quote: “The Holy Writings!” exclaimed one of his tempters, “is everything then to be judged by them? Who can understand them till the church has interpreted them?”
claims.claim_text: One of Jerome's interlocutors responded that Scripture could not be understood except through the church's interpretation.

verification.rationale: Represents the standard medieval Catholic position that Scripture must be interpreted through the church; consistent with the exchange as transmitted by Bonnechose.

claims.quote: “Are the traditions of men more worthy of faith than the gospel of our Saviour?” replied Jerome. “Paul did not exhort those to whom he wrote to listen to the traditions of men, but said, ‘Search the Scriptures.’ ”
claims.claim_text: Jerome replied that the traditions of men are not more worthy of faith than the gospel, citing the apostle Paul's exhortation to "Search the Scriptures."

verification.rationale: Consistent with Jerome's scriptural stance as transmitted by Bonnechose. (The 'Search the Scriptures' phrase is John 5:39, attributed to Jesus, not Paul, in the biblical text.)

claims.quote: “Heretic!” was the response, “I repent having pleaded so long with you. I see that you are urged on by the devil.”(151)
claims.claim_text: Jerome's interlocutor responded by calling him a heretic and saying he was urged on by the devil.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the hostile exchanges recorded in the traditional account (Bonnechose) of the attempts to make Jerome recant.

claims.quote: Erelong sentence of condemnation was passed upon him. He was led out to the same spot upon which Huss had yielded up his life.
claims.claim_text: Jerome was sentenced to death and led out for execution to the same spot where Huss had been burned.

verification.rationale: Jerome was condemned as a relapsed heretic and burned on 30 May 1416 at the same place where Hus had been executed.

sources.title: Jerome of Prague (Wikipedia) – same spot as Hus

claims.quote: He went singing on his way, his countenance lighted up with joy and peace.
claims.claim_text: Jerome went singing to his execution, his countenance lit with joy and peace.

verification.rationale: Poggio and Foxe record that Jerome sang on the way to execution and met death with a joyful countenance.

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: When the executioner, about to kindle the pile, stepped behind him, the martyr exclaimed, “Come forward boldly; apply the fire before my face. Had I been afraid, I should not be here.”
claims.claim_text: When the executioner stepped behind him to avoid Jerome's sight, Jerome told him to come forward boldly and apply the fire before his face, as he was not afraid.

verification.rationale: This famous saying at the stake is preserved in the eyewitness/traditional accounts of Jerome's death (Poggio; Foxe; Bonnechose).

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: “Lord, Almighty Father,” he cried, “have pity on me, and pardon me my sins; for Thou knowest that I have always loved Thy truth.”(152) His voice ceased, but his lips continued to move in prayer.
claims.claim_text: Jerome's last words as the flames rose were a prayer asking God's pity and pardon, affirming he had always loved God's truth.

verification.rationale: Jerome sang/prayed as the fire was lit, per Poggio's eyewitness account; the specific final prayer is transmitted via Bonnechose.

sources.title: Poggio Bracciolini, Letter to Leonardo Aretino (Elfinspell)

claims.quote: When the fire had done its work, the ashes of the martyr, with the earth upon which they rested, were gathered up, and like those of Huss, were thrown into the Rhine.
claims.claim_text: After Jerome's execution, his ashes and the surrounding earth were gathered and thrown into the Rhine, as had been done with Huss's remains.

verification.rationale: As with Hus, Jerome's ashes were cast into the Rhine to prevent veneration, per the standard accounts.

claims.quote: So perished God’s faithful light-bearers. But the light of the truths which they proclaimed,—the light of their heroic example,—could not be extinguished.
claims.claim_text: The text asserts that although Huss and Jerome perished, the light of the truths they proclaimed and their heroic example could not be extinguished.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/rhetorical claim about the enduring influence of their example; not empirically testable as framed.

claims.quote: The execution of Huss had kindled a flame of indignation and horror in Bohemia. It was felt by the whole nation that he had fallen a prey to the malice of the priests and the treachery of the emperor.
claims.claim_text: Huss's execution kindled widespread indignation and horror in Bohemia, where the nation felt he had fallen prey to the malice of priests and the treachery of the emperor.

verification.rationale: Hus's execution provoked national outrage in Bohemia and is widely regarded as a trigger of the Hussite movement and wars.

claims.quote: He was declared to have been a faithful teacher of the truth, and the council that decreed his death was charged with the guilt of murder.
claims.claim_text: The Bohemian nation declared Huss a faithful teacher of truth and charged the Council of Constance with the guilt of murder for his death.

verification.rationale: Control-sample item: the checkable core holds. Hundreds of Bohemian and Moravian nobles sent a formal protest (Sept 1415) to Constance defending Hus as a good man unjustly killed and denouncing the execution.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – protest of the Bohemian nobles

claims.quote: By the papal edicts the writings of Wycliffe had been condemned to the flames. But those that had escaped destruction were now brought out from their hiding-places, and studied in connection with the Bible, or such parts of it as the people could obtain, and many were thus led to accept the reformed faith.
claims.claim_text: Wycliffe's writings had been condemned to the flames by papal edicts, but surviving copies were brought out and studied with the Bible after Huss's death, leading many to accept the reformed faith.

verification.rationale: Wycliffe's works were condemned and ordered burned (notably at Constance, which also ordered his remains exhumed), yet his and Hus's writings continued to circulate and shape Bohemian reform.

claims.quote: The pope and the emperor united to crush out the movement, and the armies of Sigismund were hurled upon Bohemia.
claims.claim_text: The pope and Emperor Sigismund united to crush the Hussite movement, and Sigismund's armies invaded Bohemia.

verification.rationale: The papacy proclaimed crusades and Sigismund led repeated invasions of Bohemia during the Hussite Wars (1419–1434).

claims.quote: Ziska, who soon after the opening of the war became totally blind, yet who was one of the ablest generals of his age, was the leader of the Bohemians.
claims.claim_text: Jan Ziska, who became totally blind soon after the war began, was one of the ablest generals of his age and led the Bohemians.

verification.rationale: Jan Zizka, already one-eyed, lost his remaining eye in 1421 (early in the war) and led the Hussites while totally blind; he is regarded as an outstanding, undefeated commander.

sources.title: Hussite Wars (Wikipedia) – Zizka blind, brilliant general

claims.quote: Trusting in the help of God and the righteousness of their cause, that people withstood the mightiest armies that could be brought against them. Again and again the emperor, raising fresh armies, invaded Bohemia, only to be ignominiously repulsed.
claims.claim_text: The Bohemians repeatedly repulsed Emperor Sigismund's forces despite him raising fresh armies to invade Bohemia multiple times.

verification.rationale: The Hussites repelled multiple anti-Hussite crusades and imperial invasions across the 1420s–1430s.

claims.quote: A few years after the opening of the war, the brave Ziska died; but his place was filled by Procopius, who was an equally brave and skilful general, and in some respects a more able leader.
claims.claim_text: A few years after the war began, Jan Ziska died and was succeeded by Procopius, an equally brave and skillful general considered in some respects more able.

verification.rationale: Zizka died in 1424 (five years into the war) and Prokop the Great became the leading Hussite commander thereafter.

claims.quote: The enemies of the Bohemians, knowing that the blind warrior was dead, deemed the opportunity favorable for recovering all that they had lost. The pope now proclaimed a crusade against the Hussites, and again an immense force was precipitated upon Bohemia, but only to suffer terrible defeat.
claims.claim_text: After Ziska's death, the pope proclaimed a crusade against the Hussites, sending an immense force into Bohemia that suffered a terrible defeat.

verification.rationale: Successive papally proclaimed crusades were launched against the Hussites in the later 1420s and 1431 and were defeated, notably at Usti (1426) and Domazlice (1431).

claims.quote: Another crusade was proclaimed. In all the papal countries of Europe, men, money, and munitions of war were raised. Multitudes flocked to the papal standard, assured that at last an end would be made of the Hussite heretics. Confident of victory, the vast force entered Bohemia.
claims.claim_text: A further crusade was proclaimed, drawing men, money, and munitions from papal countries across Europe, and a large confident force entered Bohemia.

verification.rationale: The fifth anti-Hussite crusade of 1431 assembled a large international force that invaded Bohemia.

claims.quote: “The crusaders were in greatly superior force, but instead of dashing across the stream, and closing in battle with the Hussites whom they had come so far to meet, they stood gazing in silence at those warriors.”(153)
claims.claim_text: According to a quoted account (source 153), the crusading force, though greatly superior in numbers, stood gazing in silence at the Hussites across a river rather than attacking.

verification.rationale: At Domazlice (14 Aug 1431) the larger crusader army did not engage; according to the traditional account, on hearing the approaching Hussites the crusaders panicked and fled.

claims.quote: Then suddenly a mysterious terror fell upon the host. Without striking a blow, that mighty force broke and scattered, as if dispelled by an unseen power.
claims.claim_text: The crusading army suddenly broke and scattered without a fight, seized by what is described as a mysterious terror.

verification.rationale: At Domazlice the crusaders broke and fled in panic (reportedly on hearing the Hussite battle-hymn and wagons) before real battle was joined; GC frames the panic as supernatural.

sources.title: Battle of Domazlice (Wikipedia) – crusaders fled in panic

claims.quote: Great numbers were slaughtered by the Hussite army, which pursued the fugitives, and an immense booty fell into the hands of the victors, so that the war, instead of impoverishing, enriched the Bohemians.
claims.claim_text: Great numbers of fleeing crusaders were slaughtered by the pursuing Hussite army, and the Hussites captured immense booty, so that the war enriched rather than impoverished the Bohemians.

verification.rationale: The routed crusaders were pursued and slaughtered and abandoned a large baggage train captured by the Hussites at Domazlice.

claims.quote: A few years later, under a new pope, still another crusade was set on foot. As before, men and means were drawn from all the papal countries of Europe.
claims.claim_text: A few years later, under a new pope, another crusade against the Hussites drew men and resources from papal countries across Europe.

verification.rationale: Multiple anti-Hussite crusades were mounted; GC's sequencing partly overlaps with the same 1431 campaign. The general pattern of repeated European crusades is accurate, though GC's enumeration is loose.

claims.quote: Full forgiveness of the most heinous crimes was insured to every crusader. All who died in the war were promised a rich reward in heaven, and those who survived were to reap honor and riches on the field of battle.
claims.claim_text: Crusaders in this later campaign against the Hussites were promised full forgiveness of crimes, a heavenly reward if they died, and honor and riches if they survived.

verification.rationale: Crusade indulgences promising full remission of sins were standard inducements for the anti-Hussite crusades.

claims.quote: The Hussite forces fell back before them, thus drawing the invaders farther and farther into the country, and leading them to count the victory already won. At last the army of Procopius made a stand, and turning upon the foe, advanced to give them battle.
claims.claim_text: The Hussite forces initially retreated to draw the crusaders deep into Bohemia before Procopius's army made a stand and advanced to give battle.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the Domazlice campaign (1431) under Prokop the Great, where the crusaders advanced into Bohemia before the Hussite relief army routed them.

sources.title: Battle of Domazlice (Wikipedia) – Prokop the Great

claims.quote: As the sound of the approaching force was heard, even before the Hussites were in sight, a panic again fell upon the crusaders. Princes, generals, and common soldiers, casting away their armor, fled in all directions.
claims.claim_text: Before the Hussite army was even in sight, panic fell upon the crusaders and princes, generals, and soldiers fled in all directions, discarding their armor.

verification.rationale: The traditional account of Domazlice holds that the crusaders panicked and fled at the mere sound of the approaching, singing Hussite army.

claims.quote: In vain the papal legate, who was the leader of the invasion, endeavored to rally his terrified and disorganized forces. Despite his utmost endeavors, he himself was swept along in the tide of fugitives. The rout was complete, and again an immense booty fell into the hands of the victors.
claims.claim_text: The papal legate leading this crusade failed to rally his panicked troops and was himself swept along by the fleeing soldiers; the rout was complete and the Hussites again took immense booty.

verification.rationale: Cardinal Giuliano Cesarini, the papal legate at Domazlice, could not stop the flight and had to flee, abandoning the baggage to the Hussites.

sources.title: Battle of Domazlice (Wikipedia) – Cardinal Cesarini fled

claims.quote: The invaders were smitten with a supernatural terror. He who overthrew the hosts of Pharaoh in the Red Sea, who put to flight the armies of Midian before Gideon and his three hundred, who in one night laid low the forces of the proud Assyrian, had again stretched out His hand to wither the power of the oppressor.
claims.claim_text: The panic that struck the crusading armies is interpreted as a supernatural terror sent by God, comparable to the divine defeats of Pharaoh's army, Midian before Gideon, and the Assyrian army.

verification.rationale: Theological interpretation attributing the panic to divine intervention; not empirically testable.

claims.quote: The papal leaders, despairing of conquering by force, at last resorted to diplomacy. A compromise was entered into, that while professing to grant to the Bohemians freedom of conscience, really betrayed them into the power of Rome.
claims.claim_text: After failing to conquer the Hussites by force, papal leaders resorted to diplomacy and entered a compromise that professed to grant freedom of conscience but actually betrayed the Bohemians to Rome's power.

verification.rationale: Interpretive characterization of the Compactata as a deceptive betrayal; the underlying facts (turn to negotiation) are covered elsewhere, but the 'betrayal' framing is evaluative.

claims.quote: The Bohemians had specified four points as the condition of peace with Rome: The free preaching of the Bible; the right of the whole church to both the bread and the wine in the communion, and the use of the mother tongue in divine worship; the exclusion of the clergy from all secular offices and authority; and in cases of crime, the jurisdiction of the civil courts over clergy and laity alike.
claims.claim_text: The Bohemians specified four conditions for peace with Rome: free preaching of the Bible, communion in both bread and wine with vernacular worship, exclusion of clergy from secular offices, and equal civil court jurisdiction over clergy and laity in crime.

verification.rationale: The Four Articles of Prague were: (1) free preaching of the Word; (2) communion in both kinds; (3) clergy dispossessed of secular power/property; (4) punishment of public/mortal sins. GC's version (via Wylie) rewords them, adding 'use of the mother tongue' (not one of the articles) to article 2 and recasting article 4 as civil-court jurisdiction over clergy, deviating from the actual articles.
verification.modern_consensus: The authentic Four Articles are free preaching, utraquist communion, clerical poverty/loss of secular power, and punishment of mortal sins.
verification.period_historiography: Wylie's History of Protestantism rendered the articles in the reworded form GC reproduces.

sources.title: Compacts of Basel / Four Articles of Prague (Wikipedia)

defense.argument: The strongest honest case is that White conveys the Four Articles of Prague in substance, not caricature. Her article 1 (free preaching of the Bible) matches the authentic first article closely; her article 3 (exclusion of clergy from secular offices and authority) matches the authentic third article (clergy dispossessed of secular power/property) closely. Her article 4 (civil-court jurisdiction over clergy and laity alike in cases of crime) is a defensible paraphrase of the authentic fourth article's core thrust — that public and mortal sins be punished by the proper authority sparing no one regardless of rank, i.e. ending clerical immunity from secular justice. So three of the four are substantively faithful. Two deviations remain, however: White folds 'the use of the mother tongue in divine worship' into the utraquist communion article, and vernacular worship — though a genuine, closely-associated Hussite demand — was not one of the four codified articles; and she narrows 'mortal/public sins' to 'crime.' Because these are real rewordings (traceable to Wylie's rendering) rather than fabrications, the claim is period-typical paraphrase — anachronistic is correct, and the two genuine departures block a clean 'supported.'

impact.reasoning: Consistent with the defender's assessment, this is the lowest-severity item: three of the four articles GC lists (free preaching, clerical exclusion from secular power, civil jurisdiction over clergy in cases of crime) are close, defensible paraphrases of the authentic Four Articles of Prague, and only the folding of 'use of the mother tongue' into the communion article and the narrowing of 'mortal/public sins' to 'crime' are genuine departures. The article list is local color supporting the chapter's account of the Compactata negotiations, not a claim the chapter's persecution/martyrdom/providence argument depends on, so centrality is low. Deleting or correcting the wording would not change the chapter's narrative that Rome negotiated in bad faith. Anachronistic status reflects Wylie's own rewording, faithfully reproduced rather than freshly distorted by White.

claims.quote: The papal authorities at last “agreed that the four articles of the Hussites should be accepted, but that the right of explaining them, that is, of determining their precise import, should belong to the council—in other words, to the pope and the emperor.”(155)
claims.claim_text: The papal authorities agreed to accept the Hussites' four articles but reserved the right to interpret their precise meaning to the council, effectively the pope and emperor.

verification.rationale: The Compactata of Basel (1436) accepted the four articles but weakened them with vague, restrictive clauses controlled by the council/church, matching GC's substance.

sources.title: Compacts of Basel (Wikipedia) – articles weakened by clauses

claims.quote: On this basis a treaty was entered into, and Rome gained by dissimulation and fraud what she had failed to gain by conflict; for, placing her own interpretation upon the Hussite articles, as upon the Bible, she could pervert their meaning to suit her own purposes.
claims.claim_text: Rome gained through dissimulation and fraud, via the treaty's interpretive clause, what it had failed to gain by military conflict, being able to pervert the meaning of the Hussite articles as it did the Bible.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/motive claim characterizing Rome's diplomacy as fraud; not empirically testable, though the factual basis (interpretive reservation) is verified at GC-06-163.

claims.quote: A large class in Bohemia, seeing that it betrayed their liberties, could not consent to the compact. Dissensions and divisions arose, leading to strife and bloodshed among themselves.
claims.claim_text: A large class in Bohemia rejected the compact with Rome as a betrayal of their liberties, causing internal dissension and bloodshed among the Bohemians.

verification.rationale: The radical Hussites (Taborites/Orphans) rejected compromise, leading to civil war between moderate Utraquists and radicals, culminating at the Battle of Lipany (1434).

claims.quote: In this strife the noble Procopius fell, and the liberties of Bohemia perished.
claims.claim_text: Procopius died in the internal strife following the compact with Rome, and Bohemia's liberties subsequently perished.

verification.rationale: Prokop the Great was killed at the Battle of Lipany (1434), a civil war between Hussite factions; the radical Hussite cause collapsed thereafter. (Lipany preceded the formal Compactata of 1436, so GC's ordering is slightly compressed.)

sources.title: Hussite Wars (Wikipedia) – Prokop killed at Lipany 1434

claims.quote: Sigismund, the betrayer of Huss and Jerome, now became king of Bohemia, and regardless of his oath to support the rights of the Bohemians, he proceeded to establish popery.
claims.claim_text: Sigismund, who had betrayed Huss and Jerome's safe-conducts, became king of Bohemia and, disregarding his oath to support Bohemian rights, proceeded to establish popery there.

verification.rationale: Sigismund was finally accepted as king of Bohemia in 1436 after the Compactata and moved to restore Catholic authority, provoking renewed discontent.

sources.title: Compacts of Basel (Wikipedia) – Sigismund accepted as king 1436

claims.quote: But he had gained little by his subservience to Rome. For twenty years his life had been filled with labors and perils. His armies had been wasted and his treasuries drained by a long and fruitless struggle; and now, after reigning one year, he died, leaving his kingdom on the brink of civil war, and bequeathing to posterity a name branded with infamy.
claims.claim_text: Sigismund gained little from his subservience to Rome: after twenty years of labors and perils and a long fruitless struggle that wasted his armies and treasuries, he died after reigning as king of Bohemia for one year, leaving the kingdom on the brink of civil war.

verification.rationale: Sigismund entered Prague as accepted king in Aug 1436 and died in Dec 1437, reigning in Bohemia roughly a year (about 15 months); his long struggle over Bohemia had lasted some two decades.

sources.title: Compacts of Basel (Wikipedia) – Sigismund's brief Bohemian reign, died 1437

claims.quote: Tumults, strife, and bloodshed were protracted. Again foreign armies invaded Bohemia, and internal dissension continued to distract the nation. Those who remained faithful to the gospel were subjected to a bloody persecution.
claims.claim_text: After Sigismund's death, prolonged tumults, foreign invasions, and internal dissension continued in Bohemia, and those faithful to the gospel were subjected to bloody persecution.

verification.rationale: Bohemia remained troubled by internal strife and religious conflict for decades after 1437, with persecution of dissenters (later the Unity of Brethren).

claims.quote: As their former brethren, entering into compact with Rome, imbibed her errors, those who adhered to the ancient faith had formed themselves into a distinct church, taking the name of “United Brethren.”
claims.claim_text: Bohemians who adhered to the original faith, distinct from those who compromised with Rome, formed a separate church called the "United Brethren."

verification.rationale: The Unitas Fratrum (Unity of the Brethren / Bohemian Brethren) was founded at Kunvald in 1457 by those seeking a stricter apostolic church apart from the compromising Utraquists.

claims.quote: This act drew upon them maledictions from all classes. Yet their firmness was unshaken. Forced to find refuge in the woods and caves, they still assembled to read God’s word and unite in His worship.
claims.claim_text: The formation of the United Brethren drew condemnation from all classes, forcing them to take refuge in woods and caves where they continued to assemble for worship.

verification.rationale: The early Unity of Brethren were persecuted and at times forced to meet secretly in remote places (the 'Brethren of the caves'/latibulum period).

claims.quote: Through messengers secretly sent out into different countries, they learned that here and there were “isolated confessors of the truth, a few in this city and a few in that, the object, like themselves, of persecution; and that amid the mountains of the Alps was an ancient church, resting on the foundations of Scripture, and protesting against the idolatrous corruptions of Rome.”(156)
claims.claim_text: The United Brethren, through secretly sent messengers, learned of isolated confessors of the truth in various cities and of an ancient church in the Alps resting on Scripture and protesting Rome's corruptions.

verification.rationale: The Brethren sent out emissaries seeking fellow believers and made contact with the Alpine Waldenses, from whom they later sought episcopal orders.

claims.quote: This intelligence was received with great joy, and a correspondence was opened with the Waldensian Christians.
claims.claim_text: The United Brethren received the news of the Alpine church with great joy and opened correspondence with the Waldensian Christians.

verification.rationale: The Unity of Brethren established contact with the Waldenses and in 1467 obtained episcopal ordination through a Waldensian bishop.

sources.title: Unitas Fratrum / Moravian Church (Wikipedia) – Waldensian orders 1467

claims.quote: “Their lot was cast in evil days, but ... they remembered the words first uttered by Huss, and repeated by Jerome, that a century must revolve before the day should break. These were to the Taborites [Hussites] what the words of Joseph were to the tribes in the house of bondage: ‘I die, and God will surely visit you, and bring you out.’ ”(157)
claims.claim_text: A quoted source (reference 157) states that the Hussites/Taborites remembered a saying first uttered by Huss and repeated by Jerome that a century must pass before the day should break, likened to Joseph's words to the tribes in Egypt.

verification.rationale: The saying that 'a century must revolve before the day should break,' attributed to Huss and Jerome, belongs to the reformer-prophecy tradition (akin to the goose/swan legend). Modern scholarship holds Hus uttered no such datable prophecy; it was elaborated after the fact, notably by Luther in the 1530s. GC transmits it via a 19th-c source (157).
verification.modern_consensus: Hus's supposed prophecy of a coming reformer after a hundred years is a post hoc construction, not an authentic contemporary utterance.
verification.period_historiography: 19th-c Protestant historians (Wylie; Gillett) reported the prophecy as genuine and fulfilled in Luther.

sources.title: Jan Hus (Wikipedia) – the prophecy is a later construction
Quote of the Week – Hus a Goose, Luther a Swan (discussion of the legend)

defense.argument: Two steelman moves exist but neither rescues the claim. First, the item is framed as a QUOTATION from a 19th-century source (ref. 157) reporting what the Taborites 'remembered,' so White transmits her source rather than asserting the prophecy's authenticity in her own voice. Second, Hussite expectation of eventual vindication genuinely circulated, and the 'goose' wordplay (Hus = husa/goose in Czech) is authentic — Hus alluded to the goose in his own letters. But the specific, datable prophecy that 'a century must revolve before the day should break,' attributed by name to Huss and repeated by Jerome, belongs to the reformer-prophecy tradition elaborated after the fact (notably by Luther in the 1530s, mapping Hus's goose onto Luther's swan across roughly a century). Modern scholarship treats this hundred-years prophecy as a post-hoc construction, not an authentic contemporary utterance. Crucially, unlike the anonymous saying at GC-06-010, this one is specifically named and specifically addressed and rejected by scholarship, so it cannot be softened to merely 'unverifiable.' It matched White's 19th-c sources (Wylie, Gillett) and is wrong by modern standards — the textbook definition of anachronistic. Defense fails.

impact.reasoning: The specific, datable 'a century must revolve before the day should break' prophecy, named to both Huss and Jerome, belongs to the post-hoc reformer-prophecy tradition (elaborated by Luther in the 1530s mapping the Hus-goose onto the Luther-swan); unlike the anonymous saying at GC-06-010, this one is concretely attributed and concretely rejected by modern scholarship, so it cannot be softened past anachronistic. Centrality is high because this quotation functions as the chapter's rhetorical capstone, explicitly and repeatedly (see also GC-06-176) tying Huss's martyrdom forward to its 'fulfillment' in Luther and the Reformation -- exactly the linkage the book's central thesis chain needs between the pre-Reformation and Reformation narrative_arcs. It is not load-bearing for chapter 6 itself: the chapter's account of Huss's death, the Hussite wars, and the United Brethren's survival stands on its own supported evidentiary base without this specific prophecy. As an inherited quotation from a named 19th-century source (Gillett, via ref. 157), it reflects period scholarship rather than authorial invention.

claims.quote: “The closing period of the fifteenth century witnessed the slow but sure increase of the churches of the Brethren. Although far from being unmolested, they yet enjoyed comparative rest. At the commencement of the sixteenth century, their churches numbered two hundred in Bohemia and Moravia.”(158)
claims.claim_text: A quoted source (reference 158) states that by the close of the fifteenth century the churches of the Brethren slowly but surely increased, and by the start of the sixteenth century their churches numbered two hundred in Bohemia and Moravia.

verification.rationale: The Unity of Brethren grew substantially by 1500 (tens of thousands of members in Bohemia and Moravia); the figure of about two hundred congregations is consistent with the standard estimates of their late-15th-c expansion.

sources.title: Unitas Fratrum / Moravian Church (Wikipedia) – growth by 1500

claims.quote: “So goodly was the remnant which, escaping the destructive fury of fire and sword, was permitted to see the dawning of that day which Huss had foretold.”
claims.claim_text: A quoted source concludes that a goodly remnant of the Brethren, escaping fire and sword, was permitted to see the dawning of the day Huss had foretold.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/rhetorical conclusion invoking Huss's (legendary) prophecy; not empirically testable, and the underlying prophecy is addressed at GC-06-174.
